Professor View 01 / 27
Business English -- 96 Lessons

Murielle Xavier

Business English for HR leadership in international meetings

B1 (Intermediate) Guarulhos, SP Executive HR Manager — Minerva Foods 60 min · Online
Progresso Geral0%
Velocidade:
Nome

Murielle Xavier

Idade

44 anos

Cidade

Guarulhos, SP — trabalha no Itaim Bibi

Profissão

Gerente Executiva de RH — HR Business Partner na Minerva Foods (operações em 16 países)

Nível

B1 (Intermediário) — produção oral funcional e vocabulário de RH fortes, desenvolvendo estruturas intermediárias (present perfect, modais)

Foco

Business English de RH: listening de sotaques (australiano, árabe-inglês), reuniões executivas, apresentação de indicadores

Frequência

2x por semana (terças e quartas, 22h) — Online

Total de Lessons

96 aulas (60 min)

Personagem-Jornada

De: Uma executiva de RH que negocia muito bem em português, mas ainda precisa de um brasileiro ao lado para confirmar o que combinou em inglês — e foge do sotaque australiano tentando fazer leitura labial pela câmera.

Para: Uma gerente que chega à Austrália, entra nas reuniões sozinha, entende os executivos locais, apresenta seus indicadores de RH com autoridade e fecha combinados sem precisar perguntar para ninguém "o que que eu combinei?". Em 96 aulas.

Promessa Transformadora

De "esconder embaixo da mesa" a conduzir reuniões com australianos sem precisar de ninguém ao lado confirmando o que foi dito — com método, etapas visíveis e sem lacunas no caminho. A dor da Murielle não é o inglês em si: é a autonomia em tempo real, o listening de sotaques não-padrão e a velocidade das reuniões multilíngues. Cada aula prioriza compreensão auditiva e produção em cenários reais da Minerva Foods.

Forças
  • Vocabulário técnico de RH em inglês já ativo (compensation, turnover, headcount, business partner)
  • Produção oral funcional e espontânea — estrutura narrativas completas em inglês
  • Alta coragem comunicativa: já usa o inglês em reuniões e almoços com executivos estrangeiros
  • Autoconsciência pedagógica clara — "eu sinto falta de método"
  • Rotina de escuta ativa instalada (audiobooks e podcasts no trânsito)
Pontos de Melhoria
  • Listening para sotaques não-padrão — especialmente australiano e árabe-inglês
  • Lacunas gramaticais básicas convivendo com produção avançada (efeito "swiss cheese")
  • Processamento em tempo real em reuniões rápidas e multilíngues
  • Autonomia sem apoio de brasileiros — dependência de validação ainda presente
  • Preposições e collocações com interferência do português ("go back to home")

Metodologia

1. Listening de Sotaques no Centro

A maior dor da Murielle é entender australianos e árabe-inglês em reuniões rápidas. Todo listening tem âncora de atenção e treino direcionado de accent recognition (australiano, árabe, americano).

2. Cenários Reais da Minerva Foods

Reuniões executivas com a operação australiana, apresentação de turnover e acidentes, negociação de remuneração variável e grade salarial. Ela pratica o que vai fazer de verdade.

3. Autonomia em Tempo Real

O objetivo é eliminar a dependência do brasileiro ao lado. Frases de gerenciamento de reunião ("Could you slow down a bit?", "Just to confirm what we agreed...") em todas as aulas.

4. Fechar as Lacunas sem Perder o Avançado

Rewrite e drills focados nas lacunas estruturais (preposições, artigos) em contexto profissional, sem nunca reduzir a densidade da produção oral que ela já tem.

5. Método e Progressão Visível

A Murielle pediu método e ROI claro. Ritmo vivo na aula das 22h, etapas mensuráveis e checkpoints periódicos para ela enxergar a evolução rumo à imersão na Austrália.

Mapa de Personalidade

IntrovertidaExtrovertida
Aprendiz leitura/escritaAprendiz auditiva
ImpulsivaReflexiva
Tolerância ao erro baixaTolerância ao erro alta
Energia calmaEnergia dinâmica e variada
Insegura em inglêsConfiante em inglês
Foco acadêmicoFoco profissional
Lesson 01 -- Pre-class

Who Is Murielle? -- Your Professional Self-Introduction

Introducing yourself and your career to an international HR counterpart. Key words: background, report to, counterpart, stakeholder, track record, expertise, fast-paced, take on, fluent, headquarters, keep in touch. Structure: present perfect (experience, for/since) contrasted with present simple.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Background -- your professional history and training
"My background is in HR and labor relations."
Report to -- to have someone as your direct manager
"I report directly to the VP of People."
Counterpart -- a person with the same role somewhere else
"I speak with my Australian counterpart every week."
Stakeholder -- a person with an interest in a project
"I keep senior stakeholders informed at all times."
Track record -- your history of results and achievements
"I have a strong track record in talent development."
Expertise -- deep knowledge or skill in one area
"My main area of expertise is people development."
Fast-paced -- moving or changing very quickly
"I work in a fast-paced global environment."
Take on -- to accept a new task or responsibility
"I have taken on several international projects."
Fluent -- able to use a language easily and well
"I am not fully fluent, but I am improving fast."
Headquarters -- the main office of a company
"Our headquarters is in São Paulo, Brazil."
Keep in touch -- to stay in contact with someone
"Let us keep in touch after the call."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Background
Report to
Counterpart
Stakeholder
Track record
Expertise
Fast-paced
Take on
Fluent
Headquarters
Keep in touch

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Murielle has worked in HR for over twelve years, and she has been with Minerva Foods since 2019. Her background is in HR and labor relations, and her main expertise is people development. "I report to the VP of People, and I work with senior stakeholders every day," she says. Over the years, she has taken on several international projects and has built a strong track record. These days she speaks with her Australian counterpart almost every week, because the environment is very fast-paced. "I have never worked abroad, but that is my goal," she adds. When she meets a new partner, she asks: "How long have you worked in this area?"

1. Why do we say "she has worked in HR for twelve years" and not "she worked"?
A Because the action finished a long time ago.
B Because it started in the past and still continues now.
C Because it is a plan for the future.
2. "She has been with Minerva since 2019" -- what does "since" tell us?
A The starting point of a period up to now.
B The total length of the period.
C A single moment in the future.
3. Which question uses the present perfect correctly?
A "How long you work in this area?"
B "How long have you worked in this area?"
C "How long do you have worked here?"

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Perfect vs Present Simple

GRAMMAR

Use the present perfect to connect your past experience to now. Use the present simple for current facts. / Use o present perfect para ligar sua experiência ao presente; o present simple para fatos atuais.

FormUseExample
have / has + past participleExperience up to now (no exact time)I have led global HR projects.
... for + periodHow long the situation has lastedI have worked here for twelve years.
... since + start pointWhen the situation startedI have been here since 2019.
... ever / neverLife experience (or none)I have never worked abroad.
Present simpleFacts and routines true now: I report to the VP. / My expertise is people development.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I have in HR for over twelve years."
"I have been with Minerva 2019."
"I have taken several international projects."
"I directly to the VP of People."
"I have worked abroad, but that is my goal."
"How long have you in this area?"

Stage 2: Put the Introduction in Order

Order

Put the steps of a strong professional introduction in the correct order.

?Share your experience: "I have worked in HR for twelve years."
?Suggest staying in contact: "Let us keep in touch."
?Greet the person and say "Nice to meet you."
?Ask about them: "How long have you worked in HR?"
?Say your name, your role, and what you are in charge of.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence and then record yourself saying it.

Hi, I'm Murielle. It's a pleasure to meet you.
Use this to introduce yourself professionally.
I have worked in HR for over twelve years.
Use this to describe your experience.
I report to the VP of People in Brazil.
Use this to explain your position.
I have taken on several international HR projects.
Use this to talk about your track record.
How long have you worked in this area?
Use this to ask about someone's experience.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each professional situation.

A partner asks about your experience. What sounds most professional?
A "I work in HR since long time."
B "I have worked in HR for over twelve years, mostly in people development."
C "I am working in HR twelve years ago."
Someone asks: "Who do you report to?" You answer:
A "I have reported the VP of People."
B "I report directly to the VP of People in Brazil."
C "My report is the VP of People."
You want to ask a new partner about their experience. The best question is:
A "Since when you work in HR?"
B "How long have you worked in HR, and what is your expertise?"
C "How much time you have of HR?"
You want to close the conversation and keep the contact. You say:
A "It has been a pleasure. Let us keep in touch after the call."
B "Okay, we finish now, bye."
C "We keep in touch since the call."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are on a global HR call with Australian executives. A new partner asks: "Can you tell us a bit about yourself?" Introduce yourself: your name and role, how long you have worked in HR, your background and expertise, one project you have taken on, and one goal. Use the present perfect for your experience. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 1

1Hi, I'm Murielle. It's a pleasure to meet you.To greet and introduce yourself.
2I have worked in HR for over twelve years.To describe your experience.
3I report to the VP of People and lead a small team.To explain your position.
4My expertise is people development and talent.To talk about your area of strength.
5It's been a pleasure. Let's keep in touch.To close and keep contact.
Lesson 02 -- Pre-class

This Is What I Do -- Describing Your HR Role

Explaining your role, your team and your processes to an international colleague. New words: oversee, liaise with, handle, support, implement, recruit, retain, department, streamline, onboarding, workload, cross-functional. Grammar: defining relative clauses (who / which / that) + the passive for HR processes + frequency adverbs.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Oversee -- to manage and be responsible for an area or team (supervisionar)
"I oversee the team that handles compensation."
Liaise with -- to work closely and exchange information with another team
"I liaise with the managers who lead each unit."
Handle -- to be responsible for and deal with a task or process (lidar com)
"Recruitment is handled by my department."
Support -- to help an area by giving it the resources it needs
"We support Finance, Legal, and Sustainability."
Implement -- to put a plan, policy or system into action (implementar)
"The policies that we implement are reviewed every year."
Recruit -- to find, attract and hire new people for a company
"We recruit new talent for the operations in Chile."
Retain -- to keep your best employees so they stay in the company (reter)
"We work hard to retain our key people."
Department -- a section of a company responsible for one main function
"The people department manages talent and culture."
Streamline -- to make a process simpler, faster and more efficient (otimizar)
"I streamline any process that is too slow."
Onboarding -- the process of welcoming and training new employees (integracao)
"New hires are onboarded within their first week."
Workload -- the amount of work a person or team has to do (carga de trabalho)
"When the workload is heavy, the tasks are shared."
Cross-functional -- involving people from several different departments
"We work on cross-functional projects across the company."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with the correct definition.

Oversee
Liaise with
Handle
Support
Implement
Recruit
Retain
Department
Streamline
Onboarding
Workload
Cross-functional

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Murielle is the HR manager who oversees the business partner team at Minerva Foods. Recruitment is handled by her department, and new hires are onboarded within their first week. She works closely with the managers who lead the operations in Chile and Australia. The policies that her team implements are reviewed every year. When the workload is heavy, the tasks are shared across the team. She usually streamlines any process that is too slow. "It depends on the business unit," she often says.

1. In "the managers who lead the operations", why do we use "who"?
A Because the action is in the past.
B Because "who" adds information about people (a relative clause).
C Because "managers" is singular.
2. "Recruitment is handled by her department" is an example of:
A The passive, because the focus is on the process, not on who does it.
B A question in the present simple.
C A future plan.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "New hires is onboarded within a week."
B "New hires are onboarded within a week."
C "New hires are onboard within a week."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Relative Clauses & the Passive for Processes

GRAMMAR

Use relative clauses (who / which / that) to add information, and the passive (is / are + past participle) to describe processes when the doer is not important.

FormUseExample
... who + verbRelative clause for people (pessoas)the managers who lead each unit
... which / that + verbRelative clause for things / processes (coisas)the policy that we implement
is / are + past participlePassive: focus on the process (voz passiva)Recruitment is handled by my team.
Is / Are + subject + past participle?Passive questionAre new hires onboarded quickly?
always / usually / often / sometimes / rarely / neverFrequency adverb before the main verb: I usually streamline slow processes.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I work with the managers lead our regional units."
"Recruitment handled by my department."
"New hires onboarded within a week."
"This is the policy we implement every year."
"The workload shared across the team when it is heavy."
"I streamline any process is too slow."

Stage 2: Put the Explanation in Order

Order

Put the steps of explaining your role to a new colleague in the correct order.

?Describe your team and who you report to.
?Offer to help and suggest staying in touch.
?Greet the colleague and welcome them.
?Explain the processes that are handled by your department.
?Say your role and what you oversee.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence and then record yourself saying it.

I oversee the team that handles compensation and benefits.
Use a relative clause to describe your team.
Recruitment and onboarding are handled by my department.
Use the passive to describe a process.
I liaise with the managers who lead each unit.
Use a relative clause to say who you work with.
New hires are onboarded within their first week.
Use the passive for the onboarding process.
I streamline the processes that slow my team down.
Use a relative clause with a new verb.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each professional situation.

A new colleague asks what your team does. What is the best answer?
A "I do many HR things every day."
B "I oversee the team that handles recruitment and onboarding."
C "I am oversee the team who handle recruitment."
You want to describe a process without saying who does it. You say:
A "Recruitment is handled by a dedicated team."
B "Recruitment handle by a dedicated team."
C "Recruitment is handle by a dedicated team."
Which relative clause is correct?
A "The managers which lead the units send reports."
B "The managers who lead the units send reports."
C "The managers who leads the units send reports."
Which passive sentence is correct?
A "New hires is onboarded in their first week."
B "New hires onboard are in their first week."
C "New hires are onboarded in their first week."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

A new international colleague joins Minerva Foods and asks: "So, what does your team actually do?" Describe your role, the team you oversee, who you liaise with, and the processes that are handled by your department. Use at least one relative clause (who / which / that), one passive form, and four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 2

1I oversee the team that handles compensation.Eu supervisiono a equipe que cuida da remuneracao.
2Recruitment is handled by my department.O recrutamento e conduzido pelo meu departamento.
3New hires are onboarded within a week.Os novos contratados sao integrados em uma semana.
4I liaise with the managers who run each unit.Eu me articulo com os gestores que lideram cada unidade.
5It depends on the workload that week.Depende da carga de trabalho naquela semana.
Lesson 03 -- Pre-class

Looking Back -- Past Experiences & Career Milestones

Telling your professional story: past roles, projects, and the big moments of your career. New words: milestone, achievement, promotion, merger, restructuring, launch, lead a project, previous, spearhead, turnaround, downsizing. Grammar: past simple vs past continuous for narrating milestones, plus present perfect vs past simple for talking about experience.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Milestone -- an important moment or stage in a career (marco)
"Leading that merger was a real milestone for me."
Achievement -- a good result you reach through effort and hard work
"Keeping the whole team together was my biggest achievement."
Promotion -- a move to a higher, more senior position (promoção)
"I have had two promotions since I joined the company."
Merger -- when two companies join to become one (fusão)
"While the merger was happening, we protected our key people."
Restructuring -- a major reorganization of how a company works
"I was leading the restructuring when the results improved."
Launch -- to start a new program, product, or project
"We launched a new onboarding program across the region."
Lead a project -- to guide and be in charge of a project
"I have led three big projects over the last decade."
Previous -- earlier; the one that came before the current one
"In my previous role, I was managing recruitment for the region."
Spearhead -- to lead an important new effort or initiative (encabeçar)
"I spearheaded the new diversity program last year."
Turnaround -- a change from loss or failure to success (recuperação)
"That difficult year became a genuine turnaround for us."
Downsizing -- reducing the number of employees to cut costs (enxugamento)
"During the downsizing, we supported everyone who left."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Milestone
Achievement
Promotion
Merger
Restructuring
Launch
Lead a project
Previous
Spearhead
Turnaround
Downsizing

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

"I have worked in HR for fifteen years, and I have led three major restructurings. My career started in a small consultancy. While I was learning the basics, my manager gave me my first real project. In 2018, while the company was going through a merger, I spearheaded the downsizing. It was the hardest year of my career. Last year, I led the biggest turnaround of all: while the leadership team was debating the numbers, we kept our best people and morale slowly improved. I have never forgotten that achievement."

1. "While the company was going through a merger, I spearheaded the downsizing." Why is "was going" used here?
A It is past continuous: a longer background action while a shorter action happened.
B It is present simple: a habit that happens every day.
C It is a plural noun.
2. "I have worked in HR for fifteen years." Why present perfect and not past simple?
A Because the action finished long ago and has no link to now.
B Because the experience started in the past and continues up to now.
C Because it is a question about the future.
3. Which sentence correctly contrasts experience with a finished moment?
A "I have led the biggest turnaround last year."
B "I have led three turnarounds. Last year I led the biggest one."
C "I have led three turnarounds. Last year I have led the biggest one."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Past Simple vs Past Continuous (and Present Perfect for experience)

GRAMMAR

Use o past continuous para a ação longa de fundo e o past simple para a ação curta que aconteceu no meio. Use o present perfect para experiência sem tempo definido, e o past simple quando você diz QUANDO. (Background action vs finished action; experience vs specific moment.)

FormUse (PT)Example
was/were + verb-ingAção longa de fundo / background actionWhile I was leading the restructuring...
past simple (interruption)Ação curta no meio / shorter action...we merged two teams.
have/has + past participleExperiência, sem quando / experienceI have led three restructurings.
past simple + time markerMomento definido / finished, WHEN is saidLast year I led the biggest one.
while / when / for / everMarcadores: While the merger was happening, I spearheaded the downsizing. / Have you ever led a turnaround?

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a forma correta do verbo (past simple, past continuous ou present perfect). A pista entre parênteses é o infinitivo. (Complete with the correct form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.)

"While I the restructuring, we merged two teams. (lead)"
"I have three turnarounds in my career. (lead)"
"Last year I my biggest promotion. (get)"
"Have you ever a new program? (spearhead)"
"While the team the numbers, morale was falling. (debate)"
"I never forgotten that achievement. (present perfect)"

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de uma história de carreira na ordem correta. (Put the steps of a career story in the correct order.)

?Describe a milestone: a project you led or an effort you spearheaded.
?Say what you have learned and where you are now.
?Say when and where you started your career.
?Talk about a hard change, like a merger, a downsizing, or a turnaround.
?Mention your previous role and company.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

While I was leading the merger, I spearheaded the downsizing.
Enquanto eu liderava a fusão, encabecei o enxugamento do quadro.
I have led three major restructurings in my career.
Já liderei três grandes reestruturações na minha carreira.
Last year we completed a real turnaround.
No ano passado, concluímos uma verdadeira recuperação.
While the team was debating, morale was falling fast.
Enquanto a equipe debatia, o ânimo caía rápido.
I have never forgotten that difficult but important achievement.
Nunca esqueci aquela conquista difícil, mas importante.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

A colleague asks: "How long have you worked in HR?" You answer:
A "I worked in HR for fifteen years." (the experience is still going)
B "I have worked in HR for fifteen years."
C "I am working in HR since fifteen years."
You describe a short action inside a longer one. The correct sentence is:
A "While I led the restructuring, we merged two teams."
B "While I was leading the restructuring, we merged two teams."
C "While I was leading the restructuring, we merge two teams."
You talk about your experience, without saying exactly when. You say:
A "I have spearheaded three downsizings."
B "I have spearheaded three downsizings in 2019."
C "I was spearheading three downsizings."
You want to say when one specific milestone happened. You say:
A "I have got my promotion two years ago."
B "I got my promotion two years ago."
C "I was getting my promotion two years ago."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

Tell the story of your career so far. Where and when did you start? Which projects have you led or spearheaded? Describe one hard moment -- a merger, a downsizing, or a turnaround -- using "while" + past continuous (for example: "While I was leading the restructuring, ..."). Then say what you have learned. Use at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 3

1I have worked in HR for fifteen years.Trabalho com RH há quinze anos.
2While I was leading the merger, I spearheaded the downsizing.Enquanto eu liderava a fusão, encabecei o enxugamento do quadro.
3Last year we completed a real turnaround.No ano passado, concluímos uma verdadeira recuperação.
4I have led three major restructurings.Já liderei três grandes reestruturações.
5Looking back, every milestone has taught me something.Olhando para trás, cada marco me ensinou algo.
Lesson 04 -- Pre-class

What Is Happening Right Now -- Describing Live Situations in Meetings

Narrating live action in meetings and operations: who is presenting, what teams are doing, how projects are going. New words: currently, at the moment, ongoing, update, progress, review, on track, behind schedule, deadline, backlog, catch up. Grammar: present continuous vs present simple (now vs routine) and present perfect continuous for actions still in progress (have been working).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Currently -- at this present time; now (atualmente)
"I am currently leading two hiring projects."
At the moment -- right now; at this exact time (neste momento)
"At the moment, the team is in a meeting."
Ongoing -- still happening; not finished yet (em andamento)
"The system change is still ongoing."
Update -- new information about how something is going (atualização)
"I am sending you a quick update by email."
Progress -- forward movement toward finishing a goal (progresso)
"The team has been making good progress this week."
Review -- to look at and check something carefully (revisar)
"Two managers are reviewing the hiring plan."
On track -- going as planned, with no delay (no caminho certo)
"The project is on track for the deadline."
Behind schedule -- late; slower than the plan (atrasado)
"We are a little behind schedule on the report."
Deadline -- the date or time by which something must be finished (prazo final)
"We still have three days before the deadline."
Backlog -- a pile of work that is waiting and still needs to be done (acúmulo de trabalho)
"The team has been clearing the backlog of open roles."
Catch up -- to do work you did not finish and reach where you should be (colocar em dia)
"I am trying to catch up on my emails this morning."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Currently
At the moment
Ongoing
Update
Progress
Review
On track
Behind schedule
Deadline
Backlog
Catch up

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

It is a busy morning at Minerva Foods. Murielle usually leads the weekly meeting, but right now she is taking notes while the finance team is presenting the quarterly results. Two managers are reviewing the new hiring plan, and Sofia is updating the slides. The HR team has been working on the backlog of open roles all week, and they have been making good progress. They are not hiring new people this quarter; instead, they are training the managers they already have. The main project is still ongoing, but it is on track for the deadline. "Are we catching up on the reports?" a manager asks. "Yes, we have been catching up since Monday," Murielle answers.

1. Murielle "usually leads" the meeting, but today she "is taking" notes. Why the two different forms?
A Both describe a fixed daily routine.
B "Usually leads" is a routine (present simple); "is taking" is happening now (present continuous).
C "Is taking" is a finished past action.
2. Why do we say "The HR team has been working on the backlog all week"?
A The action started in the past and is still going on now (present perfect continuous).
B The action is a finished routine that never changes.
C The action will only start next week.
3. Which sentence correctly contrasts a routine with what is happening right now?
A "I am usually leading meetings, but now I take notes."
B "I usually lead meetings, but right now I am taking notes."
C "I usually leading meetings, but right now I taking notes."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Continuous vs Present Simple (+ Present Perfect Continuous)

GRAMMAR

Use o present continuous (am/is/are + verbo-ing) para o que acontece AGORA, e o present simple para rotinas. Para uma ação que começou no passado e ainda continua, use o present perfect continuous (have/has been + verbo-ing). (Now vs routine vs still in progress.)

FormUse (PT)Example
present simple: subject + verbRotina, sempre / routine, alwaysI usually lead the weekly meeting.
present continuous: am / is / are + verb-ingAgora, temporário / now, temporaryRight now I am taking notes / she is presenting.
negative & question (continuous)Negativa e pergunta / negative & questionWe are not hiring now. Is she presenting?
present perfect continuous: have / has been + verb-ingComeçou antes e ainda continua / started before, still goingWe have been working on the policy all morning.
now vs routine vs still in progressI usually review plans (routine), today I am reviewing the backlog (now), and I have been reviewing it since Monday (still in progress).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a forma correta: present simple (rotina), present continuous (agora) ou present perfect continuous (começou antes e continua). A pista entre parênteses é o infinitivo. (Complete with the right tense. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.)

"Right now the finance team the results. (present)"
"I usually the weekly meeting. (lead)"
"We on the backlog all morning. (work)"
"We new people right now. (not hire)"
"The team good progress since Monday. (make)"
" you catching up on the reports right now? (question)"

Stage 2: Put the Update in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de um update ao vivo na ordem correta. (Put the steps of a live update in the correct order.)

?Say what each team is doing right now.
?Finish: say you are sending a written update.
?Greet and say it is a busy morning.
?Say if the project is on track or behind schedule.
?Say what you are working on at the moment.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

Right now, the team is presenting the results.
Agora, a equipe está apresentando os resultados.
We have been working on the backlog all morning.
Estamos trabalhando no acúmulo de tarefas a manhã toda.
I usually lead meetings, but today I am taking notes.
Eu geralmente conduzo reuniões, mas hoje estou fazendo anotações.
The project is on track for the deadline.
O projeto está no caminho certo para o prazo final.
I am trying to catch up on my emails at the moment.
Estou tentando colocar meus e-mails em dia neste momento.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

You usually run the weekly meeting, but today a colleague is leading it. Someone asks what you are doing. You answer:
A "I usually take notes today."
B "I usually run the meeting, but today I am taking notes."
C "Today I take notes while my colleague leading."
Your manager asks how long you have been on the new policy. You say:
A "We are working on the new policy since Monday."
B "We have been working on the new policy since Monday."
C "We work on the new policy since Monday."
Your team is behind and needs to clear the pile of open tasks. You say:
A "We are catching up on the backlog this week."
B "We are catch up the backlog this week."
C "We catch up on backlog this week."
You give a quick status update in a meeting. You say:
A "The project on track and we making progress."
B "The project is on track and we will hit the deadline."
C "The project is on track, we falls behind schedule."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

Give a live update on your work. What do you usually do, and what are you doing differently right now? What has your team been working on this week (backlog, deadlines)? Is the project on track or behind schedule, and are you catching up? Contrast present simple (routine) with present continuous (now) and use the present perfect continuous, plus at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 4

1Right now, the team is presenting the results.Agora, a equipe está apresentando os resultados.
2We have been working on the backlog all week.Estamos trabalhando no acúmulo de tarefas a semana toda.
3I usually lead meetings, but today I am taking notes.Eu geralmente conduzo reuniões, mas hoje estou fazendo anotações.
4The project is on track for the deadline.O projeto está no caminho certo para o prazo final.
5I am catching up on my emails right now.Estou colocando meus e-mails em dia agora.
Lesson 05 -- Pre-class

Every Day vs. Right Now -- Present Simple vs Present Continuous

Separating routines from live action in HR meetings: what you always do vs what is happening right now, plus the stative verbs that never take -ing. New words: routine, agenda, attend, in charge of, assign, escalate, follow up, recurring, one-off, notice, prefer, involve. Grammar: present simple (routines, facts, stative verbs) vs present continuous (now, temporary).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Routine -- a regular, fixed way of doing things (rotina)
"My morning routine starts at eight o'clock."
Agenda -- the list of topics for a meeting (pauta / agenda)
"Let me send you the agenda for tomorrow."
Attend -- to go to and take part in a meeting (comparecer / participar)
"I attend the regional meeting every Monday."
In charge of -- responsible for something or someone (responsável por)
"I am in charge of the recruitment budget."
Assign -- to give a task or role to a person (atribuir / designar)
"We assign one recruiter to each open role."
Escalate -- to pass a problem to a higher level (escalar / encaminhar)
"People escalate difficult cases to me."
Follow up -- to check on something later and act (dar seguimento / acompanhar)
"The recruiter follows up with the candidate every week."
Recurring -- happening again and again, regularly (recorrente)
"We have a recurring meeting every Monday."
One-off -- happening only once, not regular (pontual / único)
"This new process is a one-off test, not permanent."
Notice -- to see or realize something (perceber / notar)
"I notice that we work better when we talk."
Prefer -- to like one thing more than another (preferir)
"I prefer the shorter process so far."
Involve -- to include as a necessary part (envolver / incluir)
"Our hiring process involves four steps."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Routine
Agenda
Attend
In charge of
Assign
Escalate
Follow up
Recurring
One-off
Notice
Prefer
Involve

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

Every week at Minerva Foods follows a clear routine. Murielle usually attends the regional meeting on Monday, and she is in charge of the recruitment budget. The team normally assigns one recruiter to each role, and that recruiter follows up with the candidate every week. Difficult cases escalate to Murielle. But this week is different. Right now, they are testing a shorter process, so Murielle is following up with the candidates herself. The new process involves only two steps — it is a one-off test this month, not a recurring change. "I prefer the shorter one," she says. "I notice that we make faster decisions." She adds the topic to the agenda for the next call.

1. Why does the text say "Murielle usually attends" but "she is following up... herself" this week?
A Both describe finished past actions.
B "Usually attends" is a routine (present simple); "is following up" is happening now (present continuous).
C "Is following up" is a permanent habit.
2. Why do we say "The new process involves only two steps" and not "is involving"?
A "Involve" is a stative verb, so it stays in the present simple even for now.
B "Involve" can never be used in a question.
C The action is finished and will not happen again.
3. Which sentence correctly contrasts a routine with what is happening right now?
A "I am usually assigning roles, but this week I assign them myself."
B "I usually assign roles, but this week I am following up myself."
C "I usually assigning roles, but this week I following up myself."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Simple vs Present Continuous (+ Stative Verbs)

GRAMMAR

Use o present simple (subject + verb) para rotinas e fatos, e o present continuous (am/is/are + verbo-ing) para o que acontece AGORA. Alguns verbos de estado (stative verbs: prefer, notice, involve, know, want) ficam SEMPRE no present simple, mesmo para agora. (Routine vs now vs stative.)

FormUse (PT)Example
present simple: subject + verbRotina, fatos / routine, factsI usually run the Monday meeting.
present continuous: am / is / are + verb-ingAgora, temporário / now, temporaryThis week Liam is running it.
question & negative (simple)Do / does para rotina / routineDo you attend it? We do not assign it.
stative verbs: prefer, notice, involve, know, wantNunca com -ing / never take -ingI prefer email. It involves four steps. (não "am preferring")

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a forma correta: present simple (rotina/stative) ou present continuous (agora). A pista entre parênteses é o infinitivo. (Complete with the right tense. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.)

"Every Monday, I the regional meeting. (attend)"
"But this week, Liam the meeting. (run)"
"Our hiring process four steps. (involve)"
"Right now, we a shorter process. (test)"
"I the shorter process. (prefer)"
" you attend this meeting every week? (question)"

Stage 2: Put the Description in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de uma descrição de rotina + mudança na ordem correta. (Put the steps in the correct order.)

?Say what is different this week.
?Finish: say which option you prefer.
?Start with your usual weekly routine.
?Explain how many steps the new process involves.
?Say what you are in charge of.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

Every Monday, I attend the regional meeting.
Toda segunda-feira, eu compareço à reunião regional.
This week, I am covering for my director.
Esta semana, estou substituindo meu diretor.
The process involves four steps.
O processo envolve quatro etapas.
Right now, we are testing a shorter process.
Agora, estamos testando um processo mais curto.
I prefer the shorter one because it is faster.
Eu prefiro o mais curto porque é mais rápido.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

A colleague asks what you do every Monday. You describe your routine:
A "Every Monday, I am attending the regional meeting."
B "Every Monday, I attend the regional meeting."
C "Every Monday, I attends the regional meeting."
Someone asks what is different this week. You answer:
A "This week, we are testing a shorter process."
B "This week, we test a shorter process usually."
C "This week, we testing a shorter process."
Your manager asks which option you like more. You say:
A "I am preferring the shorter one."
B "I prefer the shorter one."
C "I am prefer the shorter one."
You want to ask a colleague about their routine. You ask:
A "Are you attend this meeting every week?"
B "Do you attend this meeting every week?"
C "Do you attending this meeting every week?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

Describe your normal work week and one thing that is different right now. What do you usually do (routine, agenda, attend, in charge of, assign)? What are you doing differently at the moment? How many steps does one of your processes involve, and which option do you prefer? Contrast the present simple (routine and stative verbs) with the present continuous (now), and use at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 5

1Every Monday, I attend the regional meeting.Toda segunda-feira, eu compareço à reunião regional.
2This week, I am covering for my director.Esta semana, estou substituindo meu diretor.
3The process involves four steps.O processo envolve quatro etapas.
4Right now, we are testing a shorter process.Agora, estamos testando um processo mais curto.
5I prefer the shorter one because it is faster.Eu prefiro o mais curto porque é mais rápido.
Lesson 06 -- Pre-class

Planning Ahead -- Future HR Projects with Going To

Talking about decided future plans and evidence-based predictions for HR projects with be + going to + base verb. New words: goal, target, forecast, budget, roadmap, quarter, priority, expand, set up, aim to, deliver, strategy. Grammar: going to for plans already decided and predictions from evidence (affirmative, negative, question).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Goal -- a result you plan to achieve (meta / objetivo)
"Our main goal this quarter is faster hiring."
Target -- a specific number you aim for (alvo / meta numérica)
"Our target is ten new hires this quarter."
Forecast -- a statement of what will probably happen (previsão)
"The forecast shows strong hiring next year."
Budget -- the money planned for a project (orçamento)
"The budget for the new team is already approved."
Roadmap -- a plan showing the steps over time (roteiro / plano)
"I am going to send you the roadmap on Friday."
Quarter -- a three-month business period (trimestre)
"We are going to expand the team next quarter."
Priority -- what you do first because it matters most (prioridade)
"Please look at the priorities before our call."
Expand -- to grow or make bigger (expandir)
"We are going to expand the team by ten people."
Set up -- to create or establish something (montar / estabelecer)
"We are going to set up a new onboarding program."
Aim to -- to intend to do something (ter como objetivo)
"We aim to have every new hire ready in two weeks."
Deliver -- to produce a promised result (entregar / cumprir)
"We are going to deliver the growth plan this year."
Strategy -- a plan to reach a big goal (estratégia)
"Our strategy is to promote from inside."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Goal
Target
Forecast
Budget
Roadmap
Quarter
Priority
Expand
Set up
Aim to
Deliver
Strategy

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

At Minerva Foods, the HR team has a clear plan for next quarter. Their main goal is faster hiring, and the budget is already approved. "We are going to expand the team by ten people," Murielle explains. "Our target is to have every new hire ready in two weeks." Their strategy is to promote from inside, so they are not going to hire externally for senior roles. Instead, they are going to set up a faster onboarding program. The numbers support the plan: applications are up thirty percent, so the forecast is strong. "Based on this evidence, next year is going to be a great year," Murielle says. She is going to deliver the full roadmap on Friday, and she asks the team to review the priorities first.

1. Why does Murielle say "We are going to expand the team"?
A It is a finished action from last year.
B It is a plan already decided for the future (be + going to + base verb).
C It is a daily routine that never changes.
2. Why does the text say "next year is going to be a great year"?
A It is a prediction based on evidence now (applications are up).
B It is an order given to the team.
C It describes a habit that happens every year.
3. Which sentence is a correct negative plan with going to?
A "We not going to hire externally."
B "We are not going to hire externally."
C "We are not going to hiring externally."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- 'Going To' for Plans and Predictions

GRAMMAR

Use be + going to + verbo base para planos já decididos e para previsões baseadas em evidência. Nunca misture "will" com "going to", e nunca use -ing depois de "going to". (Decided plans and evidence-based predictions.)

FormUse (PT)Example
am / is / are + going to + base verbPlano já decidido / decided planWe are going to expand the team.
going to (prediction)Previsão de evidência / prediction from evidenceApplications are up, so hiring is going to be strong.
negative: be + not + going toPlano negativo / negativeWe are not going to hire externally.
question: Be + subject + going toPerguntar sobre um plano / ask about a planAre you going to launch it in March?

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com 'be + going to + verbo base'. A pista entre parênteses é o infinitivo. (Complete with going to. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.)

"Next quarter, we the team. (expand)"
"I the roadmap on Friday. (send)"
"Applications are up, so next year strong. (be)"
"For senior roles, we externally. (not hire)"
"We a new onboarding program. (set up)"
" you going to launch it in March? (question)"

Stage 2: Put the Plan in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de uma apresentação de plano na ordem correta. (Put the steps of a plan pitch in the correct order.)

?Explain the strategy: promote from inside.
?Finish: say when you are going to send the roadmap.
?Start with your main goal for the quarter.
?Give a prediction based on the numbers.
?State the target and confirm the budget.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

We are going to expand the team next quarter.
Vamos expandir a equipe no próximo trimestre.
Our goal is faster hiring and the budget is approved.
Nossa meta é contratar mais rápido e o orçamento está aprovado.
The numbers are up, so next year is going to be strong.
Os números subiram, então o próximo ano vai ser forte.
We are not going to hire externally for senior roles.
Não vamos contratar externamente para cargos sêniores.
I am going to send the roadmap on Friday.
Vou enviar o roteiro na sexta-feira.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

You present a decided plan for next quarter. You say:
A "We will going to expand the team."
B "We are going to expand the team."
C "We going to expand the team."
The numbers are strong and you make a prediction. You say:
A "Based on the data, next year is going to be strong."
B "Based on the data, next year going to be strong."
C "Based on the data, next year is going to being strong."
You want to ask a colleague about their launch plan. You ask:
A "You going to launch it in March?"
B "Are you going to launch it in March?"
C "Do you going to launch it in March?"
You state your onboarding target in a meeting. You say:
A "Our target is to have every new hire ready in two weeks."
B "Our target is have every new hire ready in two weeks."
C "Our target going to have every new hire in two weeks."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

Present your HR plan for next quarter. What is your main goal and target? What is your budget and strategy? What are you going to do, and what are you not going to do? Give one prediction based on evidence (for example, "applications are up, so..."). Use "be going to + base verb" and at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 6

1We are going to expand the team next quarter.Vamos expandir a equipe no próximo trimestre.
2Our goal is faster hiring and the budget is approved.Nossa meta é contratar mais rápido e o orçamento está aprovado.
3The numbers are up, so next year is going to be strong.Os números subiram, então o próximo ano vai ser forte.
4We are not going to hire externally for senior roles.Não vamos contratar externamente para cargos sêniores.
5I am going to send the roadmap on Friday.Vou enviar o roteiro na sexta-feira.
Lesson 07 -- Pre-class

More, Less, the Best -- Comparatives and Superlatives

Comparing HR data and performance across regions with comparatives (lower than, more efficient than) and superlatives (the lowest, the most cost-effective), plus irregular forms (better/best, worse/worst). New words: rate, turnover, benchmark, increase, decrease, gap, ranking, metric, efficient, cost-effective, outperform, trend.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Rate -- a measured number or index (taxa / índice)
"Our turnover rate is nine percent."
Turnover -- how many employees leave and are replaced (rotatividade)
"Turnover in Australia is lower than in Europe."
Benchmark -- a standard used to compare (referência / parâmetro)
"Europe is our benchmark for cost per hire."
Increase -- to go up or rise (aumentar)
"Applications increased by thirty percent."
Decrease -- to go down or fall (diminuir)
"Our cost per hire decreased this year."
Gap -- a difference between two things (lacuna / diferença)
"There is a big gap between our fastest and slowest region."
Ranking -- a list ordered by performance (classificação / ranking)
"Australia is at the top of the ranking."
Metric -- a number you measure (métrica / indicador)
"Time to hire is our most important metric."
Efficient -- doing well with little waste (eficiente)
"Our process is more efficient than last year."
Cost-effective -- good value for the money (bom custo-benefício)
"Europe is the most cost-effective region."
Outperform -- to do better than others (superar)
"Australia outperforms the benchmark on engagement."
Trend -- a general direction of change (tendência)
"The trend is positive: turnover keeps falling."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Rate
Turnover
Benchmark
Increase
Decrease
Gap
Ranking
Metric
Efficient
Cost-effective
Outperform
Trend

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

Murielle is preparing the quarterly review. Her metrics tell a clear story. The turnover rate in Australia is lower than in Europe — in fact, it is the lowest in the company. On cost, however, the picture is different: Australia's cost per hire is higher than the European benchmark, so Europe is still the most cost-effective region. The biggest gap is onboarding time. The good news is that the process is more efficient than last year, applications have increased, and costs have decreased. Retention is better than before, and the trend is positive. On engagement, Australia outperforms the benchmark and sits at the top of the ranking.

1. Why does the text say "lower than" but "the lowest"?
A They mean exactly the same thing.
B "Lower than" compares two things; "the lowest" is the top of a whole group.
C "The lowest" compares only two regions.
2. Why is it "the most cost-effective" and not "the cost-effectivest"?
A "Cost-effective" is a long adjective, so it takes "the most", not "-est".
B Long adjectives always take "-est".
C It should be "more cost-effective".
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "Retention is more better than before."
B "Retention is better than before."
C "Retention is the better than before."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Comparatives and Superlatives

GRAMMAR

Adjetivos curtos: -er + than (comparativo) e the -est (superlativo). Adjetivos longos: more + than e the most. Irregulares: good → better → the best; bad → worse → the worst. (Comparatives compare two; superlatives pick the top of a group.)

TypeComparativeSuperlative
short adjective (low, fast)lower thanthe lowest
long adjective (efficient)more efficient thanthe most efficient
good / bad (irregular)better / worse thanthe best / the worst
degree / equalitymuch lower; almost as fast as Europe

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com o comparativo ou superlativo correto. A pista entre parênteses é o adjetivo. (Complete the comparison. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.)

"Our turnover is Europe. (low)"
"Australia has rate in the company. (low)"
"Our new process is the old one. (efficient)"
"Europe is region. (cost-effective)"
"Retention is last year. (good)"
"Where is gap? (big)"

Stage 2: Put the Comparison in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de uma análise de dados na ordem correta. (Put the steps of a data review in the correct order.)

?Point out the biggest gap between regions.
?Finish with the trend and your headline.
?Start with your best metric (the lowest turnover).
?Compare your process to last year (more efficient).
?Admit the weaker metric (cost higher than benchmark).

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

Our turnover is lower than Europe.
Nossa rotatividade é menor do que a da Europa.
Australia has the lowest rate in the company.
A Austrália tem a menor taxa da empresa.
Our process is more efficient than last year.
Nosso processo é mais eficiente do que no ano passado.
Europe is the most cost-effective region.
A Europa é a região com melhor custo-benefício.
The trend is positive and retention is better than before.
A tendência é positiva e a retenção está melhor do que antes.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

You compare two turnover numbers (9% vs 14%). You say:
A "Our turnover is more lower than Europe."
B "Our turnover is lower than Europe."
C "Our turnover is lower Europe."
You highlight the top region on cost. You say:
A "Europe is the most cost-effective region."
B "Europe is the cost-effectivest region."
C "Europe is more cost-effective region."
You compare this year to last year (good). You say:
A "This year is more good than last year."
B "This year is better than last year."
C "This year is the better than last year."
You point to the largest difference. You say:
A "The biggest gap is in onboarding time."
B "The most big gap is in onboarding time."
C "The bigger gap is in onboarding time."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

Compare your HR data to a benchmark or to last year. Which metric is your best, and which is your weakest? Where is the biggest gap? What is the trend? Use comparatives (lower than, more efficient than) and superlatives (the lowest, the most cost-effective), plus at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 7

1Our turnover is lower than Europe.Nossa rotatividade é menor do que a da Europa.
2Australia has the lowest rate in the company.A Austrália tem a menor taxa da empresa.
3Our process is more efficient than last year.Nosso processo é mais eficiente do que no ano passado.
4Europe is the most cost-effective region.A Europa é a região com melhor custo-benefício.
5The trend is positive and retention is better than before.A tendência é positiva e a retenção está melhor do que antes.
Lesson 08 -- Pre-class

How Much, How Many -- Quantifiers and Countable/Uncountable

Talking about quantities in HR reports and workforce data with quantifiers (many, much, a few, a little, a lot of, several, too much, too many, not enough) and countable vs uncountable nouns. New words: workforce, headcount, vacancy, applicant, feedback, data, absence, overtime, shortage, resource, staff, training.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Workforce -- all the people who work in a company (força de trabalho)
"Our workforce is growing this year."
Headcount -- the total number of employees (quadro / número de funcionários)
"Our headcount is two hundred people."
Vacancy -- an open job position (vaga em aberto)
"We have several open vacancies right now."
Applicant -- a person who applies for a job (candidato / postulante)
"We received many applicants last month."
Feedback -- comments about performance (feedback / retorno)
"We collected a lot of feedback from the team."
Data -- facts and numbers you analyze (dados)
"The data is clear: turnover is falling."
Absence -- time when someone is not at work (ausência / falta)
"There is too much absence this quarter."
Overtime -- extra hours worked beyond normal (horas extras)
"The team is doing too much overtime."
Shortage -- when there is not enough of something (escassez / falta)
"We have a shortage of engineers."
Resource -- money, people, or tools you use (recurso)
"We have plenty of resources for training."
Staff -- the group of people who work somewhere (equipe / pessoal)
"We do not have enough staff on the night shift."
Training -- teaching employees new skills (treinamento)
"There is not much training this quarter."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Workforce
Headcount
Vacancy
Applicant
Feedback
Data
Absence
Overtime
Shortage
Resource
Staff
Training

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

Murielle is preparing the workforce report. Right now she has several open vacancies, and last month the team received many applicants, which is good news. However, there is a problem: they do not have enough staff on the night shift, so the team is doing too much overtime. There is also a lot of absence this quarter. On the positive side, the team collected a lot of feedback, and the data is clear. They have plenty of resources for training, but only a little time to organize it. Her headline is simple: many applicants, not enough staff, and a shortage to fix first.

1. Why does the text say "many applicants" but "much overtime"?
A They mean different amounts of the same thing.
B Applicants are countable (many); overtime is uncountable (much).
C "Many" and "much" are always interchangeable.
2. Why is it "a little time" and not "a few time"?
A "Time" is uncountable here, so it takes "a little".
B "Time" is always countable.
C "A few" and "a little" mean the opposite.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "We do not have enough staffs on the night shift."
B "We do not have enough staff on the night shift."
C "We do not have enough of staff on the night shift."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Quantifiers (Countable vs Uncountable)

GRAMMAR

Substantivos contáveis (applicants, vacancies) usam many, a few, several, too many. Substantivos incontáveis (feedback, overtime, data) usam much, a little, too much. "A lot of" e "enough" funcionam com os dois. Perguntas: How many (contável) / How much (incontável).

MeaningCountable (applicants)Uncountable (feedback)
a large amountmany / a lot ofmuch / a lot of
a small amounta few / severala little
excess / lacktoo many / too few / not enoughtoo much / too little / not enough
question wordHow many vacancies?How much overtime?

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com o quantifier correto. A pista entre parênteses diz se o substantivo é contável ou incontável. (Complete with the right quantifier. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.)

"We received applicants last month. (countable, large)"
"There is overtime this quarter. (uncountable, excess)"
"We have open vacancies. (countable, small)"
"We need more time for training. (uncountable, small)"
"We do staff on the night shift. (lack)"
" feedback did we collect? (question)"

Stage 2: Put the Report in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de um relatório de força de trabalho na ordem correta. (Put the steps of a workforce report in the correct order.)

?Point out what there is too much of (overtime, absence).
?Finish with your headline and the shortage to fix first.
?Start with how many vacancies you have.
?Mention the feedback and data you collected.
?Say how many applicants applied last month.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

We received many applicants last month.
Recebemos muitos candidatos no mês passado.
There is too much overtime and not enough staff.
Há horas extras demais e pessoal insuficiente.
We collected a lot of feedback and the data is clear.
Coletamos muito feedback e os dados estão claros.
How much feedback and how many applicants did we get?
Quanto feedback e quantos candidatos recebemos?
We have a shortage of engineers this quarter.
Temos uma escassez de engenheiros neste trimestre.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

You report a big number of applicants. You say:
A "We received much applicants."
B "We received many applicants."
C "We received a much applicants."
You describe an excess of overtime. You say:
A "There is too much overtime."
B "There are too many overtime."
C "There is too many overtime."
You ask about the number of vacancies. You ask:
A "How much vacancies do we have?"
B "How many vacancies do we have?"
C "How many vacancy do we have?"
You say the night shift lacks people. You say:
A "We do not have enough staff on the night shift."
B "We do not have enough staffs on the night shift."
C "We do not have many enough staff on the night shift."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

Give a short workforce report for your area. How many vacancies and applicants do you have? How much overtime and absence? Do you have enough staff, resources, and training time? Use quantifiers (many, much, a few, a little, too much, not enough) and at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 8

1We received many applicants last month.Recebemos muitos candidatos no mês passado.
2There is too much overtime and not enough staff.Há horas extras demais e pessoal insuficiente.
3We collected a lot of feedback and the data is clear.Coletamos muito feedback e os dados estão claros.
4How much feedback and how many applicants did we get?Quanto feedback e quantos candidatos recebemos?
5We have a shortage of engineers this quarter.Temos uma escassez de engenheiros neste trimestre.
Lesson 08 -- Pre-class

How Much, How Many -- Quantifiers and Countable/Uncountable

Talking about quantities in HR reports and workforce data with quantifiers (many, much, a few, a little, a lot of, several, too much, too many, not enough) and countable vs uncountable nouns. New words: workforce, headcount, vacancy, applicant, feedback, data, absence, overtime, shortage, resource, staff, training.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Workforce -- all the people who work in a company (força de trabalho)
"Our workforce is growing this year."
Headcount -- the total number of employees (quadro / número de funcionários)
"Our headcount is two hundred people."
Vacancy -- an open job position (vaga em aberto)
"We have several open vacancies right now."
Applicant -- a person who applies for a job (candidato / postulante)
"We received many applicants last month."
Feedback -- comments about performance (feedback / retorno)
"We collected a lot of feedback from the team."
Data -- facts and numbers you analyze (dados)
"The data is clear: turnover is falling."
Absence -- time when someone is not at work (ausência / falta)
"There is too much absence this quarter."
Overtime -- extra hours worked beyond normal (horas extras)
"The team is doing too much overtime."
Shortage -- when there is not enough of something (escassez / falta)
"We have a shortage of engineers."
Resource -- money, people, or tools you use (recurso)
"We have plenty of resources for training."
Staff -- the group of people who work somewhere (equipe / pessoal)
"We do not have enough staff on the night shift."
Training -- teaching employees new skills (treinamento)
"There is not much training this quarter."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Workforce
Headcount
Vacancy
Applicant
Feedback
Data
Absence
Overtime
Shortage
Resource
Staff
Training

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

Murielle is preparing the workforce report. Right now she has several open vacancies, and last month the team received many applicants, which is good news. However, there is a problem: they do not have enough staff on the night shift, so the team is doing too much overtime. There is also a lot of absence this quarter. On the positive side, the team collected a lot of feedback, and the data is clear. They have plenty of resources for training, but only a little time to organize it. Her headline is simple: many applicants, not enough staff, and a shortage to fix first.

1. Why does the text say "many applicants" but "much overtime"?
A They mean different amounts of the same thing.
B Applicants are countable (many); overtime is uncountable (much).
C "Many" and "much" are always interchangeable.
2. Why is it "a little time" and not "a few time"?
A "Time" is uncountable here, so it takes "a little".
B "Time" is always countable.
C "A few" and "a little" mean the opposite.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "We do not have enough staffs on the night shift."
B "We do not have enough staff on the night shift."
C "We do not have enough of staff on the night shift."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Quantifiers (Countable vs Uncountable)

GRAMMAR

Substantivos contáveis (applicants, vacancies) usam many, a few, several, too many. Substantivos incontáveis (feedback, overtime, data) usam much, a little, too much. "A lot of" e "enough" funcionam com os dois. Perguntas: How many (contável) / How much (incontável).

MeaningCountable (applicants)Uncountable (feedback)
a large amountmany / a lot ofmuch / a lot of
a small amounta few / severala little
excess / lacktoo many / too few / not enoughtoo much / too little / not enough
question wordHow many vacancies?How much overtime?

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com o quantifier correto. A pista entre parênteses diz se o substantivo é contável ou incontável. (Complete with the right quantifier. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.)

"We received applicants last month. (countable, large)"
"There is overtime this quarter. (uncountable, excess)"
"We have open vacancies. (countable, small)"
"We need more time for training. (uncountable, small)"
"We do staff on the night shift. (lack)"
" feedback did we collect? (question)"

Stage 2: Put the Report in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de um relatório de força de trabalho na ordem correta. (Put the steps of a workforce report in the correct order.)

?Point out what there is too much of (overtime, absence).
?Finish with your headline and the shortage to fix first.
?Start with how many vacancies you have.
?Mention the feedback and data you collected.
?Say how many applicants applied last month.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

We received many applicants last month.
Recebemos muitos candidatos no mês passado.
There is too much overtime and not enough staff.
Há horas extras demais e pessoal insuficiente.
We collected a lot of feedback and the data is clear.
Coletamos muito feedback e os dados estão claros.
How much feedback and how many applicants did we get?
Quanto feedback e quantos candidatos recebemos?
We have a shortage of engineers this quarter.
Temos uma escassez de engenheiros neste trimestre.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

You report a big number of applicants. You say:
A "We received much applicants."
B "We received many applicants."
C "We received a much applicants."
You describe an excess of overtime. You say:
A "There is too much overtime."
B "There are too many overtime."
C "There is too many overtime."
You ask about the number of vacancies. You ask:
A "How much vacancies do we have?"
B "How many vacancies do we have?"
C "How many vacancy do we have?"
You say the night shift lacks people. You say:
A "We do not have enough staff on the night shift."
B "We do not have enough staffs on the night shift."
C "We do not have many enough staff on the night shift."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

Give a short workforce report for your area. How many vacancies and applicants do you have? How much overtime and absence? Do you have enough staff, resources, and training time? Use quantifiers (many, much, a few, a little, too much, not enough) and at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 8

1We received many applicants last month.Recebemos muitos candidatos no mês passado.
2There is too much overtime and not enough staff.Há horas extras demais e pessoal insuficiente.
3We collected a lot of feedback and the data is clear.Coletamos muito feedback e os dados estão claros.
4How much feedback and how many applicants did we get?Quanto feedback e quantos candidatos recebemos?
5We have a shortage of engineers this quarter.Temos uma escassez de engenheiros neste trimestre.
Lesson 09 -- Pre-class

When It Was Happening -- Past Continuous

Describing background and interrupted past actions in meetings and crisis moments with the past continuous (was/were + verb-ing) and past continuous + past simple (while / when). New words: incident, crisis, disruption, breakdown, complaint, handover, grievance, misconduct, dispute, investigation, witness, resolve.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Incident -- an unexpected problem or event (incidente / ocorrência)
"There was an incident with the system on Friday."
Crisis -- a serious, urgent problem (crise)
"We stayed calm during the crisis."
Disruption -- an interruption to normal work (interrupção)
"The disruption lasted two hours."
Breakdown -- a sudden failure of a system or machine (pane / colapso)
"It was a complete breakdown of the payroll system."
Complaint -- a statement that something is wrong (reclamação / queixa)
"I was handling the complaints all morning."
Handover -- passing work or responsibility to someone else (passagem / repasse)
"They were in the middle of a handover."
Grievance -- a formal complaint from an employee (queixa formal)
"I was investigating a grievance last week."
Misconduct -- bad or unacceptable behavior at work (má conduta)
"It was not a case of serious misconduct."
Dispute -- a serious disagreement between people (disputa / conflito)
"There was a dispute in the sales team."
Investigation -- the act of examining a problem carefully (investigação / apuração)
"The investigation is closed now."
Witness -- a person who saw what happened (testemunha)
"I spoke to several witnesses."
Resolve -- to solve a problem or end a conflict (resolver / solucionar)
"We resolved the dispute yesterday."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Incident
Crisis
Disruption
Breakdown
Complaint
Handover
Grievance
Misconduct
Dispute
Investigation
Witness
Resolve

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

Last Friday there was an incident at Minerva. At nine, the whole team was working normally: Liam was reviewing the payroll and two managers were interviewing candidates. Then, while they were working, the main system suddenly crashed. It was a total breakdown. When the system went down, three employees were in the middle of a handover, and they lost their notes. While the IT team was fixing the problem, Liam was handling the complaints. It took two hours, but they resolved it, and the crisis is over now.

1. Why does the text say "the team was working" and not "the team worked"?
A Because "worked" is incorrect English.
B It describes an action in progress (the background) when the incident happened.
C Because the action is in the present.
2. In "while they were working, the system crashed", which action interrupts the other?
A "crashed" (past simple) interrupts "were working" (background).
B "were working" interrupts "crashed".
C Both actions are finished and separate.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "Two managers was interviewing candidates."
B "Two managers were interviewing candidates."
C "Two managers were interview candidates."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Past Continuous

GRAMMAR

O past continuous descreve uma ação em andamento no passado. Forma: was (I/he/she/it) ou were (you/we/they) + verbo com -ing. Usamos while para a ação de fundo (longa) e o past simple para a ação que interrompe (curta): "While we were working, the system crashed."

FormStructureExample
Affirmativewas/were + verb-ing"The team was working at nine."
Negativewas/were + not + verb-ing"She was not taking notes."
QuestionWas/Were + subject + verb-ing?"Were you presenting?"
Interruptedwhile + past continuous, past simple"While we were working, it crashed."

Atenção: use was para I/he/she/it e were para you/we/they. Nunca esqueça o was/were antes do verbo-ing.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com o past continuous ou o past simple. A pista entre parênteses diz o verbo e o tipo. (Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear it.)

"At nine, the team normally. (work / background)"
"While we were working, the system . (crash / interruption)"
"Two managers candidates. (interview / background)"
"She notes at that moment. (not take / negative)"
" you presenting when the alarm rang? (question)"
"While I , I talked to several witnesses. (investigate / background)"

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de contar um incidente na ordem correta. (Put the steps of telling an incident in the correct order.)

?Say what interrupted it: while we were working, the system crashed.
?Finish with how you resolved it and that the crisis is over.
?Set the scene: at nine, the team was working normally.
?Say what you were doing during the response: I was handling the complaints.
?Give the background details: people were reviewing and interviewing.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

At nine, the team was working normally.
Às nove, a equipe estava trabalhando normalmente.
While we were working, the system crashed.
Enquanto trabalhávamos, o sistema caiu.
I was handling the complaints during the crisis.
Eu estava lidando com as reclamações durante a crise.
Two employees were arguing when the manager walked in.
Dois funcionários estavam discutindo quando o gerente entrou.
While I was investigating, I talked to several witnesses.
Enquanto eu investigava, conversei com várias testemunhas.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

You describe the scene before the incident. You say:
A "At nine, I reviewing the payroll."
B "At nine, I was reviewing the payroll."
C "At nine, I was review the payroll."
You say an action interrupted another. You say:
A "While we were working, the system crashed."
B "While we worked, the system was crashing."
C "While we work, the system crashed."
You ask a colleague about the moment of the incident. You ask:
A "What you were doing when it happened?"
B "What were you doing when it happened?"
C "What did you doing when it happened?"
You report what two people were doing. You say:
A "Two employees were arguing when the manager walked in."
B "Two employees was arguing when the manager walked in."
C "Two employees were argue when the manager walked in."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

Tell the story of a real incident, dispute, or crisis at work. Set the scene (what people were doing), say what interrupted it, what you were doing at that moment, and how you resolved it. Use the past continuous (was/were + verb-ing) and at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 9

1At nine, the team was working normally.Às nove, a equipe estava trabalhando normalmente.
2While we were working, the system crashed.Enquanto trabalhávamos, o sistema caiu.
3I was handling the complaints during the crisis.Eu estava lidando com as reclamações durante a crise.
4Two employees were arguing when the manager walked in.Dois funcionários estavam discutindo quando o gerente entrou.
5While I was investigating, I talked to several witnesses.Enquanto eu investigava, conversei com várias testemunhas.
Lesson 10 -- Pre-class

Advice and Judgment -- Modal Verbs

Giving advice, suggesting options, and stating obligations in HR conversations with modal verbs: should / shouldn't (advice), could (option), might / may (possibility), and must / have to (obligation).
