New words: recommend, policy, compliance, guideline, mentor, warning, probation, appraisal, mediate, accountable, discretion, reprimand.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Recommend -- to say that something is a good idea (recomendar / aconselhar)
"I recommend a mentor for the new analyst."
Policy -- an official company rule or plan (política / norma interna)
"We should follow the company policy first."
Compliance -- the act of following the rules and laws (conformidade / cumprimento)
"Managers must stay in full compliance."
Guideline -- advice on the correct way to do something (diretriz / orientação)
"You should check the guidelines carefully."
Mentor -- an experienced person who advises a colleague (mentor)
"You could offer him a mentor for the year."
Warning -- a formal notice about bad behavior or performance (advertência / aviso formal)
"We might have to give a formal warning."
Probation -- a trial period for a new employee (período de experiência)
"He is still on probation this quarter."
Appraisal -- a formal review of an employee's performance (avaliação de desempenho)
"Managers must complete the annual appraisal."
Mediate -- to help two sides reach an agreement (mediar)
"You could ask a senior colleague to mediate."
Accountable -- responsible and required to explain your decisions (responsável / que presta contas)
"Every manager is accountable for a fair process."
Discretion -- the freedom to decide something yourself (discrição / bom senso)
"You may use your discretion on small issues."
Reprimand -- a formal expression of disapproval (repreensão)
"A reprimand should be fair and documented."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Recommend
Policy
Compliance
Guideline
Mentor
Warning
Probation
Appraisal
Mediate
Accountable
Discretion
Reprimand

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

Liam asked for advice about an analyst on probation. My judgment: we shouldn't rush a decision. First, we should follow the policy and check the guidelines. You could offer him a mentor, because he might just need more support. Of course, we must document everything and stay in full compliance — every manager is accountable. Managers may use their discretion on small issues, but for anything serious they must speak to HR first. If the problem continues, we might have to give a formal warning, but first we should give him a fair chance.

1. Why does the text say "we should follow the policy" and not "we should to follow"?
A Because "to follow" is more polite.
B A modal verb is followed by the base verb, with no "to".
C Because "follow" is in the past.
2. What is the difference between "could offer" and "must document" here?
A "could offer" is an option; "must document" is an obligation.
B They mean exactly the same thing.
C "could offer" is stronger than "must document".
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "Every manager must to be accountable."
B "Every manager must be accountable."
C "Every manager musts be accountable."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Modal Verbs

GRAMMAR

Os verbos modais mudam a força da frase. Use should para conselho, could para uma opção, might/may para possibilidade e must/have to para obrigação. Depois do modal, o verbo fica sempre na forma base (sem to, sem -s): "You should follow the policy."

ModalUseExample
should / shouldn'tAdvice, recommendation (conselho)"You should follow the policy."
couldA soft option (uma opção)"You could offer a mentor."
might / mayPossibility (possibilidade)"He might need more support."
must / have toObligation (obrigação)"You must ensure compliance."

Atenção: o modal nunca leva -s (musts ✗) nem to (should to ✗). Para a negativa, coloque not depois do modal: should not, must not.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com o modal certo + verbo base. A pista entre parênteses diz o modal e o significado. (Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear it.)

"We the policy first. (should / advice)"
"You him a mentor. (could / option)"
"He more support. (might / possibility)"
"Every manager the appraisal. (must / obligation)"
"You a grievance. (shouldn't / advice)"
"For anything serious, you to HR first. (must / obligation)"

Stage 2: Put the Advice in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de dar um bom conselho de RH na ordem correta. (Put the steps of giving good HR advice in the correct order.)

?Suggest an option: you could offer him a mentor.
?Name the last resort: if it continues, we might have to give a warning.
?Slow down: we shouldn't rush the decision.
?State the obligation: we must document everything for compliance.
?Give the first advice: we should follow the policy and guidelines.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

You should follow the policy first.
Você deveria seguir a política primeiro.
You could offer him a mentor.
Você poderia oferecer a ele um mentor.
Managers must ensure compliance.
Os gestores precisam garantir a conformidade.
He might need more support during probation.
Ele pode precisar de mais apoio durante a experiência.
You should not ignore a grievance.
Você não deveria ignorar uma queixa formal.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

A manager asks for advice about a new employee. You say:
A "You should to check the guidelines."
B "You should check the guidelines."
C "You should checks the guidelines."
You want to suggest one possible option. You say:
A "You could offer him a mentor."
B "You could offers him a mentor."
C "You could to offer him a mentor."
You state a strong obligation for all managers. You say:
A "Managers musts complete the appraisal."
B "Managers must complete the appraisal."
C "Managers must completes the appraisal."
You give advice against a bad idea. You say:
A "You should not ignore the warning."
B "You not should ignore the warning."
C "You should ignore not the warning."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

A colleague asks for your advice on a real HR situation (a new hire, a complaint, or an appraisal). Give your professional judgment: what they should do, what they could do as an option, what might happen, and what they must do for compliance. Use should, could, might, and must with the base verb and at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 10

1You should follow the policy first.Você deveria seguir a política primeiro.
2You could offer him a mentor.Você poderia oferecer a ele um mentor.
3He might need more support.Ele pode precisar de mais apoio.
4We must document everything for compliance.Precisamos documentar tudo para a conformidade.
5You should not ignore a grievance.Você não deveria ignorar uma queixa formal.
Lesson 11 -- Pre-class

Happening Right Now -- Present Continuous

Describing actions happening right now in live meetings with the present continuous (am/is/are + verb-ing): affirmative, negative, and question forms, plus stative verbs that are not used in the continuous. New words: facilitate, attendee, presenter, poll, recap, breakout, host, chime in, walk through, multitask, sidebar, screen-share.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Facilitate -- to help a meeting run smoothly (facilitar / conduzir)
"Sofia is facilitating the session right now."
Attendee -- a person who is at a meeting (participante)
"About twenty attendees are joining online."
Presenter -- the person who is speaking or showing slides (apresentador(a))
"The presenter is walking us through the budget."
Poll -- a quick vote to collect opinions (enquete / votação rápida)
"They are running a poll about the timeline."
Recap -- a short summary of the main points (resumo / recapitulação)
"I am taking notes for the recap."
Breakout -- a small group session inside a larger meeting (sessão em grupos)
"We are moving into breakout groups now."
Host -- the person who runs the online meeting (anfitrião / organizador(a))
"The host is starting the main session."
Chime in -- to add a quick comment to a discussion (intervir / opinar)
"Two managers are chiming in with questions."
Walk through -- to explain something step by step (explicar passo a passo)
"He is walking us through the new process."
Multitask -- to do several tasks at the same time (fazer várias tarefas ao mesmo tempo)
"They are multitasking as usual."
Sidebar -- a short side conversation during a meeting (conversa paralela)
"Two colleagues are having a quick sidebar."
Screen-share -- to show your screen to the other people (compartilhar a tela)
"Nobody is screen-sharing yet."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Facilitate
Attendee
Presenter
Poll
Recap
Breakout
Host
Chime in
Walk through
Multitask
Sidebar
Screen-share

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

I am writing to you from the meeting room, so let me tell you what is happening right now. Sofia is facilitating the session, and about twenty attendees are joining online. The presenter is walking us through the new budget while he is sharing his screen. At the moment, people are running a quick poll, and two managers are chiming in with questions. I am taking notes for the recap. Nobody is multitasking too much today, and in a few minutes we are moving into breakout groups. Everything is going smoothly.

1. Why does the text say "Sofia is facilitating" and not "Sofia facilitates"?
A Because "facilitates" is incorrect English.
B It describes an action in progress right now, at the moment of speaking.
C Because the action is a fixed routine.
2. What is the structure of the present continuous here?
A am / is / are + verb-ing (is facilitating, are joining).
B was / were + verb-ing.
C will + verb.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "Twenty attendees is joining online."
B "Twenty attendees are joining online."
C "Twenty attendees are join online."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Continuous

GRAMMAR

O present continuous descreve uma ação em andamento agora. Forma: am (I), is (he/she/it), are (you/we/they) + verbo com -ing. Marcadores: right now, at the moment, currently. Verbos de estado (know, want, need, like) normalmente não usam o -ing: "I know the answer" (não "I am knowing").

FormStructureExample
Affirmativeam/is/are + verb-ing"The presenter is sharing the screen."
Negativeam/is/are + not + verb-ing"Nobody is multitasking too much."
QuestionAm/Is/Are + subject + verb-ing?"Is she facilitating now?"
Stative verbpresent simple (no -ing)"I know the answer." (not "am knowing")

Atenção: use am para I, is para he/she/it, are para you/we/they. Nunca esqueça o am/is/are antes do verbo-ing.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com o present continuous. A pista entre parênteses diz o verbo e o tipo. (Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear it.)

"Right now, the presenter the screen. (share / affirmative)"
"Twenty attendees online. (join / affirmative)"
"At the moment, I notes for the recap. (take / affirmative)"
"Nobody a screen yet. (share / with nobody)"
" she facilitating the session now? (question)"
"Two managers in with questions. (chime / affirmative)"

Stage 2: Put the Live Report in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de descrever uma reunião ao vivo na ordem correta. (Put the steps of describing a live meeting in the correct order.)

?Describe the presenter: he is walking us through the budget.
?Say what happens next: we are moving into breakout groups.
?Set the scene: the host is starting the session right now.
?Add reactions: two managers are chiming in with questions.
?Give the numbers: twenty attendees are joining online.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

The presenter is sharing the screen right now.
O apresentador está compartilhando a tela agora.
Twenty attendees are joining online.
Vinte participantes estão entrando online.
I am taking notes for the recap.
Estou anotando para o resumo.
Two managers are chiming in with questions.
Dois gerentes estão intervindo com perguntas.
Is she facilitating the session now?
Ela está conduzindo a sessão agora?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

You describe what the presenter is doing at this moment. You say:
A "The presenter sharing the screen now."
B "The presenter is sharing the screen now."
C "The presenter is share the screen now."
You report the number of people arriving. You say:
A "Twenty attendees are joining online."
B "Twenty attendees is joining online."
C "Twenty attendees are join online."
You ask a colleague what is happening right now. You ask:
A "What you are doing right now?"
B "What are you doing right now?"
C "What are you do right now?"
You state a fact you know (a stative verb). You say:
A "I know the answer to that question."
B "I am knowing the answer to that question."
C "I knowing the answer to that question."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

Imagine you are in a live meeting right now. Give a running commentary of what is happening: who is facilitating, who is presenting, what the attendees are doing, and what is happening in the chat. Use the present continuous (am/is/are + verb-ing) and at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1The presenter is sharing the screen right now.O apresentador está compartilhando a tela agora.
2Twenty attendees are joining online.Vinte participantes estão entrando online.
3I am taking notes for the recap.Estou anotando para o resumo.
4Two managers are chiming in with questions.Dois gerentes estão intervindo com perguntas.
5Is she facilitating the session now?Ela está conduzindo a sessão agora?
Lesson 12 -- Pre-class

Now vs. Always -- Present Simple vs. Present Continuous

Contrasting routines and facts (present simple) with actions happening now or temporarily (present continuous) in HR conversations, including frequency adverbs and stative verbs. New words: delegate, cover, shift, rotate, interim, standby, workflow, frequently, occasionally, temporarily, ordinarily, reassign.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Delegate -- to give a task to someone else to do (delegar)
"I usually delegate the reports to my team."
Cover -- to do someone's work while they are away (cobrir / substituir)
"This week I am covering for Sofia."
Shift -- a set period of working time (turno)
"The night shift starts at ten."
Rotate -- to change tasks or roles in turns (revezar / alternar)
"We rotate the on-call duty every month."
Interim -- temporary, for a short period (interino / provisório)
"She is the interim manager right now."
Standby -- ready to act if needed (de prontidão / em espera)
"The support team is on standby today."
Workflow -- the sequence of steps in a work process (fluxo de trabalho)
"Our normal workflow has five steps."
Frequently -- often; many times (frequentemente)
"I frequently review the payroll on Fridays."
Occasionally -- sometimes; not often (ocasionalmente)
"We occasionally work on Saturdays."
Temporarily -- for a short time only (temporariamente)
"I am temporarily working from London."
Ordinarily -- usually; as a general rule (normalmente / em geral)
"Ordinarily, the team meets on Mondays."
Reassign -- to give a task or person to a different job (reatribuir / remanejar)
"We are reassigning two analysts this week."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Delegate
Cover
Shift
Rotate
Interim
Standby
Workflow
Frequently
Occasionally
Temporarily
Ordinarily
Reassign

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

Ordinarily, I manage the payroll and I delegate the reports to my team. I usually work from the Sao Paulo office, and I frequently review the numbers on Fridays. But this week is different. Right now, I am covering for Sofia because she is away, so I am temporarily handling two roles. At the moment, we are reassigning a few analysts and rotating the on-call shifts. The support team is on standby all week. Our normal workflow still works well, but everything is moving fast today.

1. Why does the text say "I usually work" but "I am covering for Sofia"?
A The two verbs mean the same thing.
B "usually work" is a routine (present simple); "am covering" is temporary, now (present continuous).
C "am covering" is a permanent fact.
2. Which words signal a routine (present simple)?
A ordinarily, usually, frequently.
B right now, at the moment, this week.
C yesterday, last week, ago.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "Right now I usually cover for Sofia."
B "Right now I am covering for Sofia."
C "Right now I am cover for Sofia."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Simple vs. Continuous

GRAMMAR

Use o present simple para rotinas, fatos e hábitos (com advérbios como usually, frequently, ordinarily): "I delegate the reports." Use o present continuous (am/is/are + verbo-ing) para agora ou situações temporárias (com right now, at the moment, this week): "This week I am covering for Sofia." Lembre do -s na 3ª pessoa do present simple: she works.

TenseUseExample
Present simpleRoutine, fact, habit"I usually delegate the reports."
3rd person -she/she/it in present simple"She manages the London team."
Present continuousNow or temporary"This week I am covering for Sofia."
Stative verbsalways present simple"I know the process." (not "am knowing")

Atenção: rotina = present simple (usually, ordinarily); agora/temporário = present continuous (right now, this week). Não misture os dois.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com o present simple ou o present continuous. A pista entre parênteses diz o verbo e o tipo. (Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear it.)

"Ordinarily, I the reports to my team. (delegate / routine)"
"This week I for Sofia. (cover / temporary)"
"She the London team. (manage / routine)"
"Right now, we two analysts. (reassign / now)"
"We the payroll on Fridays. (review / routine)"
"I the process very well. (know / state verb)"

Stage 2: Put the Explanation in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de explicar a rotina e a mudança atual na ordem correta. (Put the steps in the correct order.)

?Signal the change: but this week is different.
?Reassure: our normal workflow still works well.
?Describe the routine: ordinarily, I delegate the reports.
?Say what is temporary now: I am covering for Sofia.
?Add frequency: I frequently review the numbers on Fridays.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

Ordinarily, I delegate the reports to my team.
Normalmente, eu delego os relatórios para a minha equipe.
This week I am covering for Sofia.
Esta semana estou substituindo a Sofia.
She manages the London team.
Ela gerencia a equipe de Londres.
Right now, we are reassigning two analysts.
Agora, estamos remanejando dois analistas.
We occasionally rotate the on-call shifts.
Ocasionalmente, revezamos os turnos de prontidão.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

You describe your normal routine. You say:
A "Ordinarily, I am delegating the reports."
B "Ordinarily, I delegate the reports."
C "Ordinarily, I delegates the reports."
You describe a temporary situation this week. You say:
A "This week I am covering for Sofia."
B "This week I cover for Sofia."
C "This week I am cover for Sofia."
You talk about what a colleague does every day. You say:
A "She manage the London team."
B "She manages the London team."
C "She is manage the London team."
You state a fact you know (a stative verb). You say:
A "I know the workflow very well."
B "I am knowing the workflow very well."
C "I knows the workflow very well."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

Explain your work life: what you ordinarily do (your routine) and what is different right now (a temporary situation this week or this month). Use the present simple for routines and the present continuous for what is happening now, plus at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1Ordinarily, I delegate the reports to my team.Normalmente, eu delego os relatórios para a minha equipe.
2This week I am covering for Sofia.Esta semana estou substituindo a Sofia.
3She manages the London team.Ela gerencia a equipe de Londres.
4Right now, we are reassigning two analysts.Agora, estamos remanejando dois analistas.
5We occasionally rotate the on-call shifts.Ocasionalmente, revezamos os turnos de prontidão.
Lesson 13 -- Pre-class

Plans and Intentions -- 'Going to' for Future Projects

Talking about plans, intentions, and predictions with going to (am/is/are + going to + verb) in HR projects, including negatives and questions. New words: rollout, initiative, pipeline, objective, allocate, draft, intend, expansion, timeline, proposal, anticipate, upcoming.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Rollout -- the launch of a new plan or system (lançamento)
"The rollout is going to be gradual."
Initiative -- a new plan or effort to solve a problem (iniciativa)
"We are going to start a hiring initiative."
Pipeline -- planned upcoming work or projects (fila de projetos / pipeline)
"We anticipate a busy pipeline next year."
Objective -- a specific goal you plan to reach (objetivo)
"Our main objective is to reduce turnover."
Allocate -- to give resources to a plan or task (alocar / destinar)
"We are going to allocate more budget."
Draft -- a first version of a document (rascunho / minuta)
"I am going to draft the proposal this week."
Intend -- to plan to do something (pretender / tencionar)
"We intend to improve the onboarding process."
Expansion -- the growth of a business or team (expansão)
"Next month we are going to start a big expansion."
Timeline -- the schedule of a plan over time (cronograma)
"We are going to review the timeline with the directors."
Proposal -- a plan you suggest for approval (proposta)
"I am going to prepare the proposal on Monday."
Anticipate -- to expect something to happen (prever / antecipar)
"We anticipate a busy quarter ahead."
Upcoming -- happening soon in the future (próximo / que está por vir)
"I want to explain our upcoming plans."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Rollout
Initiative
Pipeline
Objective
Allocate
Draft
Intend
Expansion
Timeline
Proposal
Anticipate
Upcoming

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

I want to explain our upcoming plans for the next quarter. We are going to launch a new hiring initiative in March, and we are going to allocate more budget to the training team. First, I am going to draft the proposal this week. Then we are going to review the timeline with the directors. Our main objective is to reduce turnover, so we intend to improve onboarding. We anticipate a busy pipeline, and the rollout is going to be gradual. I am not going to rush the expansion.

1. When the text says "we are going to launch a new initiative in March", what does it express?
A A finished action in the past.
B A plan or intention decided for the future (going to + verb).
C A daily routine.
2. What is the main objective of the plan?
A To reduce turnover.
B To cancel the training team.
C To rush the expansion.
3. Which sentence is the correct negative of "going to"?
A "I am going not to rush the expansion."
B "I am not going to rush the expansion."
C "I not am going to rush the expansion."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Going To (Future)

GRAMMAR

Use going to para planos e intenções já decididos e para previsões com evidência. Estrutura: am/is/are + going to + verbo base. Afirmativo: "We are going to launch the initiative." Negativo: "I am not going to rush it." Pergunta: "Are you going to draft the proposal?" (Você vai / pretende...)

FormStructureExample
Affirmativeam/is/are + going to + verb"We are going to allocate more budget."
Negativeam/is/are + not + going to"I am not going to rush the expansion."
QuestionAm/Is/Are + subject + going to"Are you going to draft the proposal?"
Prediction (evidence)going to for what you can see coming"The rollout is going to be gradual."

Atenção: use going to para planos já decididos. O verbo depois de "going to" fica sempre na forma base (sem -s, sem -ing): "She is going to lead", não "is going to leads".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a forma correta de "going to". A pista entre parênteses ajuda. (Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear it.)

"We launch a new hiring initiative. (plan, we)"
"I draft the proposal this week. (plan, I)"
"She lead the expansion project. (plan, she)"
"I rush the rollout. (negative, I)"
" you going to review the timeline today? (question)"
"They allocate two managers to the project. (plan, they)"

Stage 2: Put the Plan in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de apresentar um plano na ordem correta. (Put the steps in the correct order.)

?Give the timeline: then we are going to review it with the directors.
?Reassure: I am not going to rush the rollout.
?Introduce the plan: we are going to launch a new initiative in March.
?State the objective: our objective is to reduce turnover.
?Say the first step: first, I am going to draft the proposal.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

We are going to launch a new hiring initiative.
Vamos lançar uma nova iniciativa de contratação.
First, I am going to draft the proposal.
Primeiro, vou preparar o rascunho da proposta.
Our objective is to reduce turnover.
Nosso objetivo é reduzir a rotatividade.
I am not going to rush the rollout.
Não vou apressar o lançamento.
Are you going to review the timeline?
Você vai revisar o cronograma?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

You announce a decided plan for next quarter. You say:
A "We going to launch the initiative."
B "We are going to launch the initiative."
C "We are going to launched the initiative."
You say what you do NOT plan to do. You say:
A "I am not going to rush the rollout."
B "I am going not to rush the rollout."
C "I not going to rush the rollout."
You ask a colleague about their intention. You say:
A "You are going to draft the proposal?"
B "Are you going to draft the proposal?"
C "Do you going to draft the proposal?"
You make a prediction based on evidence (a busy pipeline). You say:
A "Next year is going to be busy."
B "Next year is going to busy."
C "Next year going to be busy."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

Present your plans for an upcoming HR project: what you and your team are going to do, your main objective, the first step you are going to take, and one thing you are not going to do. Use "going to" and at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1We are going to launch a new hiring initiative.Vamos lançar uma nova iniciativa de contratação.
2First, I am going to draft the proposal.Primeiro, vou preparar o rascunho da proposta.
3Our objective is to reduce turnover.Nosso objetivo é reduzir a rotatividade.
4I am not going to rush the rollout.Não vou apressar o lançamento.
5Are you going to review the timeline?Você vai revisar o cronograma?
Lesson 14 -- Pre-class

Decisions and Offers -- 'Will' for Spontaneous Choices

Making decisions, offers, and promises at the moment of speaking with will (will + base verb), plus predictions and the difference from going to. New words: volunteer, reassure, commit, guarantee, ensure, promptly, arrange, confirm, notify, remind, reliable, cope.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Volunteer -- to offer to do something without being asked (voluntariar-se / se oferecer)
"I will volunteer to lead the panel."
Reassure -- to make someone feel less worried (tranquilizar)
"I will reassure the team about the audit."
Commit -- to promise to do something (comprometer-se)
"I will commit to the new deadline."
Guarantee -- to promise something will happen (garantir)
"I guarantee the team will cope well."
Ensure -- to make sure something happens (assegurar / garantir)
"I will ensure everything runs smoothly."
Promptly -- quickly, without delay (prontamente)
"I will notify you promptly."
Arrange -- to organize or plan something (organizar / arranjar)
"I will arrange the documents today."
Confirm -- to say something is definitely true (confirmar)
"I will confirm the schedule this afternoon."
Notify -- to officially tell someone (notificar / avisar)
"I will notify the candidates right away."
Remind -- to help someone remember (lembrar / relembrar)
"I will remind everyone about the meeting."
Reliable -- that you can trust to do well (confiável)
"She is reliable and always delivers."
Cope -- to deal with a difficult situation (lidar / dar conta)
"The team will cope well with the pressure."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Volunteer
Reassure
Commit
Guarantee
Ensure
Promptly
Arrange
Confirm
Notify
Remind
Reliable
Cope

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

Sofia is sick and cannot run the interviews tomorrow. Do not worry — I will handle it. I will step in and lead the panel myself. I will confirm the room this afternoon, and I will notify the candidates promptly. I reassure you: I will not cancel anything. I guarantee the team will cope well, because we are reliable. I will ensure everything runs smoothly, and I will send you a short update tonight.

1. When the writer says "I will handle it", what does "will" express here?
A A plan decided a long time ago.
B A decision or offer made at the moment of speaking.
C A past action.
2. Which sentence is a promise?
A "I will not cancel anything."
B "Sofia is sick."
C "We are reliable."
3. What form of the verb comes after "will"?
A The -ing form: "I will handling."
B The base form: "I will handle."
C The -s form: "She will handles."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Will (Decisions & Offers)

GRAMMAR

Use will para decisões, ofertas e promessas feitas na hora de falar, e para previsões de opinião (I think...). Estrutura: will + verbo base (igual para todas as pessoas). Negativo: will not (won't). Pergunta: "Will you help me?" Compare: going to = plano já decidido antes; will = decisão no momento.

UseStructureExample
Spontaneous decisionwill + base verb"I will handle it."
Offer / promisewill + base verb"I will send you an update."
Negativewill not / won't + base verb"I will not cancel anything."
Prediction (opinion)I think + will"I think the auditors will be satisfied."

Atenção: depois de "will" o verbo fica sempre na forma base, sem -s e sem -ing: "She will lead", não "will leads" nem "will leading".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a forma correta de "will". A pista entre parênteses ajuda. (Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear it.)

"Do not worry, I it now. (decision)"
"I the candidates right away. (offer)"
"I promise I anything. (promise, negative)"
" you help me with the audit? (question)"
"I the documents today. (offer)"
"I think the auditors satisfied. (prediction)"

Stage 2: Put the Offer in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de oferecer ajuda numa emergência na ordem correta. (Put the steps in the correct order.)

?Give the next step: I will confirm the room this afternoon.
?Promise: I will not cancel anything.
?Reassure: do not worry, I will handle it.
?Notify people: I will notify the candidates promptly.
?Offer to step in: I will lead the panel myself.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

Do not worry, I will handle it.
Não se preocupe, eu cuido disso.
I will notify the candidates right away.
Vou avisar os candidatos imediatamente.
I promise I will not cancel anything.
Prometo que não vou cancelar nada.
Will you help me with the audit?
Você me ajuda com a auditoria?
I guarantee the team will cope well.
Garanto que a equipe vai dar conta bem.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

A colleague just told you Sofia is sick. You decide to help now. You say:
A "I am going to handle it." (you decided long ago)
B "Do not worry, I will handle it."
C "I handle it yesterday."
You want to make a promise. You say:
A "I promise I will not cancel anything."
B "I promise I not will cancel anything."
C "I promise I will cancelling anything."
You ask a colleague for help. You say:
A "You will help me with the audit?"
B "Will you help me with the audit?"
C "Do you will help me with the audit?"
You give your opinion about a future result. You say:
A "I think the auditors will be satisfied."
B "I think the auditors will satisfied."
C "I think the auditors will be satisfy."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

Imagine a colleague at work has a sudden problem (a sick teammate, a tight audit, a missing report). Offer your help on the spot: what you will do, what you promise you will not do, and one question you will ask. Use "will" and at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1Do not worry, I will handle it.Não se preocupe, eu cuido disso.
2I will notify the candidates right away.Vou avisar os candidatos imediatamente.
3I promise I will not cancel anything.Prometo que não vou cancelar nada.
4Will you help me with the audit?Você me ajuda com a auditoria?
5I guarantee the team will cope well.Garanto que a equipe vai dar conta bem.
Lesson 15 -- Pre-class

Bigger, Better, Different -- Comparatives in Global HR

Comparing HR data and teams with comparative adjectives: short (bigger than), long (more efficient than), irregular (better/worse), and equality (as ... as). New words: significant, slightly, considerably, roughly, margin, disparity, comparable, ratio, competitive, narrow, outweigh, discrepancy.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Significant -- meaningful or large enough to matter (significativo)
"The gap between the two teams is significant."
Slightly -- by a small amount (levemente / um pouco)
"Turnover is slightly lower than last year."
Considerably -- by a large amount (consideravelmente)
"The branch is considerably more efficient."
Roughly -- approximately, about (aproximadamente)
"The costs are roughly the same in both regions."
Margin -- the difference between two amounts (margem)
"The margin between our best and worst teams is wide."
Disparity -- a big and unfair difference (disparidade)
"There is a disparity between the two departments."
Comparable -- similar enough to be compared (comparável)
"The two regions are comparable in size."
Ratio -- a relationship between two numbers (proporção / razão)
"The attrition ratio is higher in Sales."
Competitive -- as good as or better than others (competitivo)
"Our salaries are more competitive than before."
Narrow -- small in difference or width (estreito / pequeno)
"The gap between them is very narrow."
Outweigh -- to be more important than (superar / pesar mais que)
"The benefits outweigh the costs."
Discrepancy -- a difference that should not be there (discrepância)
"There is a discrepancy between the target and the result."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Significant
Slightly
Considerably
Roughly
Margin
Disparity
Comparable
Ratio
Competitive
Narrow
Outweigh
Discrepancy

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

Our two regions look very different this quarter. The South region is bigger than the North, but its turnover is higher. The North is more efficient and retains staff better than the South. The disparity between them is significant. Costs are roughly the same, so the margin in results is wider than the margin in cost. Overall, the North is the more competitive model.

1. In "The South region is bigger than the North", what kind of comparison is "bigger"?
A An equality with as ... as.
B A comparative of a short adjective (adj + -er + than).
C A superlative.
2. Which phrase uses an irregular comparative?
A "retains staff better than the South"
B "the disparity is significant"
C "roughly the same"
3. Why is "more efficient" formed with "more"?
A Because efficient is a long adjective.
B Because it is irregular.
C Because it is plural.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Comparative Adjectives

GRAMMAR

Use comparativos para comparar duas coisas. Adjetivo curto: adjetivo + -er + than (bigger than). Adjetivo longo: more + adjetivo + than (more efficient than). Irregulares: good → better, bad → worse. Igualdade: as + adjetivo + as (as high as).

TipoFormaExemplo
Adjetivo curtoadj + -er + thanTurnover is higher than last year.
Adjetivo longomore + adj + thanIt is more efficient than before.
Irregulargood→better, bad→worseResults are better than the forecast.
Igualdadeas + adj + asIt is as high as last year.

Atenção: adjetivos curtos NÃO usam more (não diga more bigger). Adjetivos longos usam more, nunca -er. Better e worse são irregulares.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. A pista entre parênteses ajuda. (Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear it.)

"Our turnover is last year. (short adj)"
"This market is ours. (long adj)"
"Our results are the forecast. (irregular)"
"The gap is last quarter. (equality)"
"Attrition is this year. (short adj)"
"The North is the South. (long adj)"

Stage 2: Put the Comparison in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de apresentar uma comparação na ordem correta. (Put the steps in the correct order.)

?Open: Here is a comparison of our two regions.
?Compare size: The South is bigger than the North.
?Compare results: But the North is more efficient than the South.
?Highlight the gap: The disparity is significant.
?Recommend: The North is the more competitive model.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

Our turnover is lower than last year.
Nossa rotatividade é menor que no ano passado.
This region is more competitive than the others.
Esta região é mais competitiva que as outras.
Our results are better than the forecast.
Nossos resultados são melhores que a previsão.
The gap is as wide as last quarter.
A diferença é tão grande quanto no trimestre passado.
The disparity between teams is significant.
A disparidade entre as equipes é significativa.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

You compare two team sizes. You say:
A "Our team is more bigger than theirs."
B "Our team is bigger than theirs."
C "Our team is the biggest than theirs."
You compare efficiency. You say:
A "The North is more efficient than the South."
B "The North is efficienter than the South."
C "The North is as efficient than the South."
You compare to last year (irregular). You say:
A "Results are gooder than last year."
B "Results are more good than last year."
C "Results are better than last year."
You say two numbers are equal. You say:
A "It is as high as last year."
B "It is as higher as last year."
C "It is more high as last year."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

Compare two real parts of your HR work: two regions, two teams, or this year versus last year. Make at least four comparisons and say which one is stronger and why. Use comparatives and at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1Our turnover is lower than last year.Nossa rotatividade é menor que no ano passado.
2This region is more competitive than the others.Esta região é mais competitiva que as outras.
3Our results are better than the forecast.Nossos resultados são melhores que a previsão.
4The gap is as wide as last quarter.A diferença é tão grande quanto no trimestre passado.
5The difference is significant.A diferença é significativa.
Lesson 16 -- Pre-class

The Best in the Room -- Superlatives for Executive HR

Naming the very best of a group with superlative adjectives: short (the highest), long (the most efficient), irregular (the best/the worst), and one of the + superlative. New words: outstanding, exceptional, leading, remarkable, unmatched, foremost, prominent, prestigious, flagship, standout, peak, utmost.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Outstanding -- extremely good; much better than others (excepcional / notável)
"She is our most outstanding manager."
Exceptional -- unusually good (excepcional)
"This was an exceptional quarter for the team."
Leading -- most important or most successful (líder / principal)
"We are the leading employer in the region."
Remarkable -- so good it deserves attention (notável / impressionante)
"The team made remarkable progress."
Unmatched -- better than anything else; with no equal (inigualável / sem igual)
"Her track record is unmatched."
Foremost -- the most important; the best-known (principal / mais importante)
"He is our foremost expert on compliance."
Prominent -- important and well-known (proeminente / de destaque)
"She is a prominent leader in the company."
Prestigious -- respected and admired (prestigioso / de prestígio)
"It is the most prestigious award in the sector."
Flagship -- the best and most important of a group (carro-chefe / principal)
"This is our flagship program."
Standout -- clearly the best (destaque)
"She was the standout candidate."
Peak -- the highest point or level (pico / auge)
"Engagement reached its peak this year."
Utmost -- the greatest possible (máximo / maior possível)
"We treat safety with the utmost care."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Outstanding
Exceptional
Leading
Remarkable
Unmatched
Foremost
Prominent
Prestigious
Flagship
Standout
Peak
Utmost

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

This year's ranking is clear. The North is our best region, with the highest retention and the lowest turnover. It is also our most efficient site. The South is the weakest region, but it made the most remarkable progress of all. Diana is our most outstanding manager; her track record is unmatched. She leads our most prestigious program and is one of the most reliable leaders we have.

1. In "the highest retention rate", what form is "highest"?
A A comparative.
B A superlative of a short adjective (the + adj + -est).
C An equality.
2. Which phrase uses an irregular superlative?
A "the North is our best region"
B "the most remarkable progress"
C "the lowest turnover"
3. Why is "most outstanding" formed with "most"?
A Because outstanding is a long adjective.
B Because it is irregular.
C Because it is plural.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Superlative Adjectives

GRAMMAR

Use superlativos para o melhor ou o pior de um grupo. Adjetivo curto: the + adjetivo + -est (the highest). Adjetivo longo: the most + adjetivo (the most efficient). Irregulares: good → the best, bad → the worst. Use sempre the antes do superlativo.

TipoFormaExemplo
Adjetivo curtothe + adj + -estThe North has the highest retention.
Adjetivo longothe most + adjShe is the most efficient manager.
Irregularthe best / the worstIt was the best quarter of the year.
Um dos melhoresone of the + superlative + pluralShe is one of the most reliable leaders.

Atenção: adjetivos curtos usam -est (não the most big). Adjetivos longos usam the most, nunca -est. Sempre use the antes do superlativo.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. A pista entre parênteses ajuda. (Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear it.)

"The North is our region. (irregular)"
"She has the retention rate. (short adj)"
"He is the manager. (long adj)"
"It was the month of the year. (irregular)"
"She is one of the leaders. (one of the...)"
"This is the team in the company. (short adj)"

Stage 2: Put the Ranking in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de apresentar um ranking na ordem correta. (Put the steps in the correct order.)

?Open: Here is this year's ranking.
?Name the best region: The North is our best region.
?Give the top metric: It has the highest retention.
?Name the standout manager: Diana is our most outstanding leader.
?Announce the award: She wins the most prestigious award.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

The North is the best region this year.
A região Norte é a melhor região este ano.
She is our most outstanding manager.
Ela é a nossa gerente mais excepcional.
It has the highest retention rate.
Tem a maior taxa de retenção.
She is one of the most reliable leaders.
Ela é uma das líderes mais confiáveis.
This is the most prestigious award.
Este é o prêmio mais prestigioso.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

You name the top region (short adjective). You say:
A "This is the most biggest region."
B "This is the biggest region."
C "This is a bigger region."
You name your top manager (long adjective). You say:
A "She is the most outstanding manager."
B "She is the outstandingest manager."
C "She is more outstanding manager."
You name the top result (irregular). You say:
A "It was the goodest result."
B "It was the most good result."
C "It was the best result."
You highlight one top leader. You say:
A "She is one of the most reliable leaders."
B "She is one of the most reliablest leaders."
C "She is one the most reliable leader."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

Talk about the best in your own HR world: your best team, your top performer, and your most successful project. Use at least four superlatives and say why each one is the best. Use at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1The North is the best region this year.A região Norte é a melhor região este ano.
2She is our most outstanding manager.Ela é a nossa gerente mais excepcional.
3It has the highest retention rate.Tem a maior taxa de retenção.
4She is one of the most reliable leaders.Ela é uma das líderes mais confiáveis.
5This is the most prestigious award.Este é o prêmio mais prestigioso.
Lesson 17 -- Pre-class

How Much, How Many -- Quantifiers for HR Data

Talking about amounts and numbers with quantifiers: many / a few (countable), much / a little (uncountable), a lot of / plenty of (both), and too much / too many / not enough. New words: plenty, scarce, abundant, numerous, minimal, ample, surplus, insufficient, countless, handful, bulk, sufficient.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Plenty -- more than enough (de sobra / bastante)
"We have plenty of candidates for the role."
Scarce -- not enough; hard to find (escasso)
"Skilled staff is scarce this year."
Abundant -- existing in large quantities (abundante)
"Feedback was abundant after the survey."
Numerous -- many; a large number (numerosos / diversos)
"We received numerous applications."
Minimal -- very small in amount (mínimo)
"The budget increase was minimal."
Ample -- more than enough; plenty (amplo / suficiente)
"We have ample time to prepare."
Surplus -- an extra amount left over (excedente / sobra)
"There is a surplus of applications."
Insufficient -- not enough (insuficiente)
"The staff is insufficient for the workload."
Countless -- too many to count (incontáveis)
"We answered countless questions."
Handful -- a small number (punhado / poucos)
"Only a handful of candidates applied."
Bulk -- the largest part of something (maior parte / grosso)
"The bulk of the budget goes to salaries."
Sufficient -- enough for a purpose (suficiente)
"We have sufficient data to decide."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Plenty
Scarce
Abundant
Numerous
Minimal
Ample
Surplus
Insufficient
Countless
Handful
Bulk
Sufficient

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

This quarter we have many open roles but not much budget. We have a few recruiters and a little time before the deadline. There is too much overtime, and morale is low. We received numerous applications, but only a handful are qualified. Skilled talent is scarce. Still, we have plenty of internal candidates and sufficient data to plan. The bulk of our effort will go to the busiest teams.

1. Why do we say "many open roles" and not "much open roles"?
A Because roles are countable.
B Because roles are uncountable.
C Because roles are a plural verb.
2. Which noun is uncountable?
A budget
B recruiters
C applications
3. What does "a handful" mean here?
A A large number.
B A small number.
C Zero.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Quantifiers (Much / Many)

GRAMMAR

Use quantifiers para falar de quantidade. Substantivos contáveis (plural): many, a few, few (many roles). Substantivos incontáveis: much, a little, little (much data). Para ambos: a lot of, plenty of. Excesso ou falta: too much / too many / not enough.

Tipo de substantivoQuantifiersExemplo
Contável (plural)many / a few / fewWe have many candidates.
Incontávelmuch / a little / littleWe have a little time.
Ambosa lot of / plenty ofWe have plenty of feedback.
Excesso / faltatoo much / too many / not enoughThere is too much overtime.

Atenção: many/a few para o que se conta (candidates); much/a little para o que não se conta (budget, time). Enough vem antes do substantivo, sem of: enough staff.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. A pista entre parênteses ajuda. (Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear it.)

"We have open roles this quarter. (countable)"
"We do not have budget. (uncountable)"
"We have qualified candidates. (countable)"
"We have time before the deadline. (uncountable)"
"There is overtime this month. (excess)"
"We do not have staff. (lack)"

Stage 2: Put the Report in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de reportar as quantidades na ordem correta. (Put the steps in the correct order.)

?Open: Here are our staffing numbers.
?Report roles: We have many open roles.
?Report budget: But we do not have much budget.
?Report candidates: We have plenty of candidates, but only a handful are qualified.
?Conclude: We have sufficient data to plan the next hires.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

We have many open roles this quarter.
Temos muitas vagas abertas neste trimestre.
We do not have much budget.
Não temos muito orçamento.
We have a few qualified candidates.
Temos alguns candidatos qualificados.
There is too much overtime.
Há hora extra demais.
We have enough data to decide.
Temos dados suficientes para decidir.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

You talk about the number of roles. You say:
A "We have much roles."
B "We have many roles."
C "We have a little roles."
You talk about the amount of budget. You say:
A "We have many budget."
B "We have much budget."
C "We have a few budget."
You talk about a small number of candidates. You say:
A "We have a little candidates."
B "We have a few candidates."
C "We have much candidates."
You talk about a lack of staff. You say:
A "We do not have enough staff."
B "We do not have enough of staff."
C "We do not have many enough staff."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

Talk about the quantities in your own HR work: your headcount, budget, applications, or workload. Say what you have too much of and not enough of. Use many, much, a few, a little, and at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1We have many open roles this quarter.Temos muitas vagas abertas neste trimestre.
2We do not have much budget.Não temos muito orçamento.
3We have a few qualified candidates.Temos alguns candidatos qualificados.
4There is too much overtime.Há hora extra demais.
5We have enough data to decide.Temos dados suficientes para decidir.
Lesson 18 -- Pre-class

Something Was Happening -- Past Continuous for Storytelling

Describing actions in progress in the past with the past continuous (was/were + verb-ing), interruptions with when/while, and negatives and questions. New words: meanwhile, suddenly, unfold, simultaneously, gradually, hectic, underway, midway, unexpectedly, interruption, commotion, chaos.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Meanwhile -- at the same time (enquanto isso)
"Meanwhile, the team was finishing the report."
Suddenly -- quickly and unexpectedly (de repente)
"Suddenly, the system crashed."
Unfold -- to happen or develop (se desenrolar / acontecer)
"The crisis was unfolding fast."
Simultaneously -- at the same time (simultaneamente)
"Two meetings were running simultaneously."
Gradually -- slowly, little by little (gradualmente)
"The team was gradually calming down."
Hectic -- very busy and chaotic (agitado / caótico)
"It was a hectic morning at the office."
Underway -- already happening (em andamento)
"The audit was already underway."
Midway -- in the middle (no meio / na metade)
"The call dropped midway through the meeting."
Unexpectedly -- in a surprising way (inesperadamente)
"The director arrived unexpectedly."
Interruption -- something that stops an activity (interrupção)
"There was a sudden interruption."
Commotion -- noisy confusion (tumulto / confusão)
"There was a commotion in the corridor."
Chaos -- complete disorder (caos)
"The office was in chaos during the audit."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Meanwhile
Suddenly
Unfold
Simultaneously
Gradually
Hectic
Underway
Midway
Unexpectedly
Interruption
Commotion
Chaos

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

Yesterday morning was completely hectic. At nine, I was preparing the audit report when the system suddenly crashed. Meanwhile, the recruiters were interviewing candidates, and two meetings were running simultaneously. While I was calling IT, the auditors arrived unexpectedly. There was a real commotion in the corridor. Gradually, things were calming down, and by eleven the crisis was no longer unfolding.

1. In "I was preparing the report when the system crashed", which action was longer and in progress?
A preparing the report
B the system crashed
C both were short
2. Why do we say "the recruiters were interviewing" and not "was interviewing"?
A Because recruiters is plural (were).
B Because it is a question.
C Because it is negative.
3. What does "meanwhile" show here?
A Two things happening at the same time.
B One thing after another.
C A future plan.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Past Continuous

GRAMMAR

Use o past continuous (was/were + verbo-ing) para uma ação em andamento no passado. Uma ação longa de fundo pode ser interrompida por uma ação curta no past simple, com while/when. Negativo: was not / were not + -ing. Pergunta: Was/Were + sujeito + -ing?

UsoFormaExemplo
Ação em andamentowas/were + verbo-ingI was preparing the report.
Interrupçãopast continuous + when + past simpleI was talking when he arrived.
Duas ações (while)while + past continuousWhile I was presenting, Ethan was writing.
Negativa / Perguntawas not / were not; Was/Were...?What were you doing?

Atenção: I/he/she/it usam was; you/we/they usam were. Depois de was/were vem o verbo com -ing, não a forma base.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. A pista entre parênteses ajuda. (Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear it.)

"At nine, I the audit report. (in progress)"
"The team candidates all morning. (plural)"
"While I IT, the auditors arrived. (background)"
"We so many questions. (negative)"
"What at 3 p.m.? (question)"
"Suddenly, the system . (interruption)"

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de contar a história na ordem correta. (Put the steps in the correct order.)

?Set the scene: At nine, I was preparing the audit report.
?Add background: Meanwhile, the team was interviewing candidates.
?The interruption: Suddenly, the system crashed.
?The reaction: While I was calling IT, the auditors arrived.
?The resolution: Gradually, things were calming down.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

I was preparing the audit report.
Eu estava preparando o relatório da auditoria.
I was presenting when the system crashed.
Eu estava apresentando quando o sistema caiu.
While I was talking, Ethan was taking notes.
Enquanto eu falava, o Ethan anotava.
We were not expecting so many questions.
Não estávamos esperando tantas perguntas.
What were you doing this morning?
O que você estava fazendo esta manhã?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

You describe an action in progress in the past. You say:
A "I was review the report."
B "I was reviewing the report."
C "I reviewing the report."
The recruiters were busy. You say:
A "The recruiters was interviewing candidates."
B "The recruiters were interviewing candidates."
C "The recruiters were interview candidates."
You combine a background action and an interruption. You say:
A "I was presenting when the system crashed."
B "I presented when the system was crashing."
C "I present when the system crashed."
You ask about a past action. You say:
A "What you were doing at 3 p.m.?"
B "What were you doing at 3 p.m.?"
C "What was you doing at 3 p.m.?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

Tell the story of a real busy or chaotic day at work. Say what you and your team were doing, what suddenly happened, and how the situation unfolded. Use the past continuous (was/were + verb-ing) and at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1I was preparing the audit report.Eu estava preparando o relatório da auditoria.
2I was presenting when the system crashed.Eu estava apresentando quando o sistema caiu.
3While I was talking, Ethan was taking notes.Enquanto eu falava, o Ethan anotava.
4We were not expecting so many questions.Não estávamos esperando tantas perguntas.
5What were you doing this morning?O que você estava fazendo esta manhã?
Lesson 19 -- Pre-class

You Should, You Could, You Might -- Modal Verbs for HR Advice

Giving advice and weighing options with modal verbs: should / should not (advice), could (option), and might (possibility), all with the base verb. New words: advisable, alternative, cautious, potential, likelihood, dilemma, weigh up, drawback, worthwhile, feasible, tentative, downside.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Advisable -- sensible; a good idea (aconselhável / recomendável)
"It is advisable to talk to HR first."
Alternative -- another option (alternativa)
"We could try an alternative approach."
Cautious -- careful to avoid risk (cauteloso / cuidadoso)
"We should be cautious with this decision."
Potential -- possible in the future (potencial)
"She has great potential as a leader."
Likelihood -- the chance that something happens (probabilidade)
"The likelihood of success is high."
Dilemma -- a difficult choice (dilema)
"We are facing a real HR dilemma."
Weigh up -- to consider carefully (ponderar / avaliar)
"You should weigh up both options."
Drawback -- a disadvantage (desvantagem)
"The main drawback is the cost."
Worthwhile -- worth the time or effort (que vale a pena)
"The training is worthwhile."
Feasible -- possible to do (viável / factível)
"The plan is feasible with more time."
Tentative -- not final; uncertain (provisório / preliminar)
"We made a tentative decision."
Downside -- the negative part (lado negativo)
"The downside is the extra workload."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Advisable
Alternative
Cautious
Potential
Likelihood
Dilemma
Weigh up
Drawback
Worthwhile
Feasible
Tentative
Downside

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

We have a real dilemma: our best analyst wants to leave. First, you should talk to her, and you should not panic. It is advisable to listen before you act. We could offer a raise, or we could propose an alternative role. She might stay if we are cautious and quick. Every option has a drawback, so we should weigh up the costs. The likelihood of keeping her is higher if we act well; a good plan is worthwhile.

1. What does "should" express in "you should talk to her"?
A Advice or a recommendation.
B A past action.
C An obligation with a fine.
2. Which sentence expresses possibility (a maybe)?
A "She might stay."
B "You should talk to her."
C "It is advisable."
3. What form of the verb comes after should, could, and might?
A The base form (no to, no -s).
B The -ing form.
C The -s form.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Modal Verbs (Should / Could / Might)

GRAMMAR

Use should para conselho ou recomendação (you should...), could para opção ou possibilidade (we could...) e might para possibilidade menos certa (they might...). Estrutura: modal + verbo base (sem to, sem -s). Negativo: should not (shouldn't).

UsoEstruturaExemplo
Conselhoshould / should not + base verbYou should talk to HR.
Opçãocould + base verbWe could offer training.
Possibilidademight + base verbThey might agree.
Regra geralmodal + verbo base (sem to, sem -s)She should decide now.

Atenção: depois de should/could/might vem o verbo base, sem to e sem -s. O negativo mais comum de conselho é should not (shouldn't).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. A pista entre parênteses ajuda. (Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear it.)

"You talk to her first. (advice)"
"We offer a flexible schedule. (option)"
"She stay if we act quickly. (possibility)"
"You ignore the feedback. (negative advice)"
"We weigh up both options. (advice)"
"They test the policy first. (option)"

Stage 2: Put the Advice in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de dar um conselho estruturado na ordem correta. (Put the steps in the correct order.)

?Understand the problem: You should talk to her first.
?Give the recommendation: It is advisable to listen before you act.
?Offer an option: We could offer a raise or a new role.
?Name the risk: But we should weigh up the drawbacks.
?Predict the outcome: She might stay if we act quickly.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

You should talk to her first.
Você deveria falar com ela primeiro.
We could offer a flexible schedule.
Poderíamos oferecer um horário flexível.
She might stay if we act quickly.
Ela pode ficar se agirmos rápido.
You should not ignore the feedback.
Você não deveria ignorar o feedback.
We should weigh up both options.
Deveríamos ponderar as duas opções.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

You give strong advice. You say:
A "You should to talk to her."
B "You should talk to her."
C "You should talking to her."
You suggest an option. You say:
A "We could offer a raise."
B "We could to offer a raise."
C "We could offers a raise."
You express a possibility. You say:
A "She might stays."
B "She might to stay."
C "She might stay."
You advise against something. You say:
A "You not should ignore it."
B "You should not ignore it."
C "You should not to ignore it."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

Advise on a real HR dilemma from your own work. Say what the person should and should not do, what they could try, and what might happen. Use should, could, might, and at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 19

1You should talk to her first.Você deveria falar com ela primeiro.
2We could offer a flexible schedule.Poderíamos oferecer um horário flexível.
3She might stay if we act quickly.Ela pode ficar se agirmos rápido.
4You should not ignore the feedback.Você não deveria ignorar o feedback.
5We should weigh up both options.Deveríamos ponderar as duas opções.
Lesson 20 -- Pre-class

Block 2 Milestone -- Fluency Review: From Data to Decisions

A milestone review of Block 2, bringing together comparatives, superlatives, quantifiers, past continuous, and modal verbs in a data-to-decisions HR presentation. New words: summarize, conclude, highlight, emphasize, articulate, coherent, concise, persuasive, takeaway, standpoint, overall, clarity.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. A tradução em português está entre parênteses. (Listen and read the example.)

Summarize -- to give the main points briefly (resumir)
"Let me summarize the key results."
Conclude -- to end with a final point (concluir)
"I conclude that the North is our best region."
Highlight -- to draw attention to something (destacar)
"I want to highlight one important trend."
Emphasize -- to give special importance to (enfatizar / ressaltar)
"I emphasize that we should act quickly."
Articulate -- to express ideas clearly (articular / expressar bem)
"She can articulate her ideas clearly."
Coherent -- clear and logical (coerente)
"The report is clear and coherent."
Concise -- short and clear (conciso)
"Keep the summary concise."
Persuasive -- able to convince others (persuasivo / convincente)
"Her argument was very persuasive."
Takeaway -- the main message to remember (conclusão / mensagem principal)
"The main takeaway is that turnover fell."
Standpoint -- a point of view (ponto de vista)
"From an HR standpoint, this is good news."
Overall -- considering everything (no geral / de modo geral)
"Overall, our results improved."
Clarity -- the quality of being clear (clareza)
"I present the data with clarity."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Combine cada palavra com a definição correta. (Match each word with the correct definition.)

Summarize
Conclude
Highlight
Emphasize
Articulate
Coherent
Concise
Persuasive
Takeaway
Standpoint
Overall
Clarity

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda as perguntas. (Read the text and answer the questions.)

This year was our most successful yet. Overall, turnover is lower than last year, and the North is our best region. We have many strong teams but little budget. Last quarter, we were losing our best analysts, which was our biggest risk. From an HR standpoint, the message is clear. We should invest in the North, and we could expand the mentoring program. My main takeaway is concise: we should act now.

1. "Turnover is lower than last year" is an example of:
A a comparative.
B a superlative.
C a modal verb.
2. "We were losing our best analysts" uses:
A the past continuous.
B a quantifier.
C a comparative.
3. "We should invest in the North" uses:
A a modal verb for advice.
B a superlative.
C the past continuous.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Block 2 Review (5 Structures)

GRAMMAR

Esta é uma aula de revisão (milestone). Você junta as 5 estruturas do Bloco 2: comparativos (lower than), superlativos (the best), quantifiers (many/much), past continuous (was/were + -ing) e modais (should/could/might). Use todas para apresentar dados e recomendar decisões.

EstruturaFormaExemplo
Comparativo-er than / more ... thanTurnover is lower than last year.
Superlativothe ...-est / the mostThe North is the best region.
Quantifiersmany/much, a few/a littleWe have many teams and little budget.
Past continuouswas/were + verbo-ingWe were losing staff last quarter.
Modaisshould/could/might + verbo baseWe should invest now.

Atenção: numa apresentação, misture as estruturas com naturalidade: compare, ranqueie, quantifique, conte o que estava acontecendo e recomende a decisão.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. A pista entre parênteses ajuda. (Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear it.)

"Overall, turnover is last year. (comparative)"
"The North is our region. (superlative)"
"We have strong teams. (quantifier)"
"Last quarter, we staff. (past continuous)"
"We invest in the North. (modal)"
"We expand the mentoring program. (option)"

Stage 2: Put the Presentation in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de uma apresentação de dados a decisões na ordem correta. (Put the steps in the correct order.)

?Summarize: Overall, turnover is lower than last year.
?Rank: The North is our best region.
?Quantify: We have many teams but little budget.
?Narrate: Last quarter, we were losing our best analysts.
?Recommend: We should invest in the North.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma. (Listen, then record yourself.)

Overall, turnover is lower than last year.
No geral, a rotatividade está menor que no ano passado.
The North is our best region.
O Norte é a nossa melhor região.
We have many strong teams but little budget.
Temos muitas equipes fortes, mas pouco orçamento.
Last quarter we were losing our best analysts.
No trimestre passado estávamos perdendo nossos melhores analistas.
We should invest in the North.
Deveríamos investir no Norte.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação. (Choose the best answer for each situation.)

You compare this year with last year. You say:
A "Turnover is more lower than last year."
B "Turnover is lower than last year."
C "Turnover is the lower than last year."
You name your top region. You say:
A "The North is the most best region."
B "The North is the best region."
C "The North is a better region."
You describe what was happening last quarter. You say:
A "Last quarter we were losing staff."
B "Last quarter we was losing staff."
C "Last quarter we losing staff."
You recommend a decision. You say:
A "We should to invest in the North."
B "We should invest in the North."
C "We should invests in the North."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo a pergunta abaixo. Não existe resposta certa ou errada. (Record yourself. There is no right or wrong answer.)

Give a full data-to-decisions presentation about your own HR work. Compare this year with last year, name your best and weakest teams, quantify your resources, describe what was happening recently, and recommend what you should and could do next. Use all five Block 2 structures and at least four new words. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 20

1Overall, turnover is lower than last year.No geral, a rotatividade está menor que no ano passado.
2The North is our best region.O Norte é a nossa melhor região.
3We have many strong teams but little budget.Temos muitas equipes fortes, mas pouco orçamento.
4Last quarter we were losing our best analysts.No trimestre passado estávamos perdendo nossos melhores analistas.
5We should invest in the North.Deveríamos investir no Norte.

IN CLASS -- Select the Lesson

01
Who Is Murielle? — Your Professional Self-Introduction
Introducing yourself to an international HR counterpart -- 31 slides
02
This Is What I Do — Describing Your HR Role
Explaining your role, your team and your routine to an international colleague -- 31 slides
03
Looking Back — Past HR Experiences & Career Milestones
Telling your professional story: past roles, projects and milestones -- 31 slides
04
What Is Happening Right Now — Describing Live Situations in Meetings
Narrating live action in meetings and operations with the present continuous -- 31 slides
05
Every Day vs. Right Now — Mastering the Contrast That Confuses Everyone
Separating routines from live action in HR meetings: present simple vs present continuous -- 31 slides
06
Planning Ahead — Talking About Future HR Projects and Strategic Decisions
Talking about decided plans and evidence-based predictions with 'going to' -- 31 slides
07
More, Less, the Best — Comparing HR Data and Business Performance
Comparing HR data and performance with comparatives and superlatives -- 31 slides
08
How Much, How Many — Talking About Quantities in HR Reports and Workforce Data
Talking about quantities with quantifiers and countable/uncountable nouns -- 31 slides
09
When It Was Happening — Describing Unfolding Situations in Meetings and Crisis Moments
Describing background and interrupted past actions with the past continuous -- 31 slides
10
Advice, Possibility, and Professional Judgment — Using Modal Verbs in HR Conversations
Giving advice, suggesting options, and stating obligations with modal verbs -- 31 slides
11
What's Happening Right Now — Present Continuous in Live Meeting Contexts
Describing actions happening now in live meetings with the present continuous -- 31 slides
12
Now vs. Always — Present Simple vs. Present Continuous in HR Conversations
Contrasting routines and facts with actions happening now or temporarily -- 31 slides
13
Plans and Intentions — 'Going to' for Future Projects in HR
Talking about plans, intentions, and predictions with going to -- 31 slides
14
Decisions and Offers — 'Will' for Spontaneous Choices in HR
Making decisions, offers, and promises at the moment of speaking with will -- 31 slides
15
Bigger, Better, Different — Comparatives in Global HR
Comparing HR data and teams with comparative adjectives (short, long, irregular, and equality) -- 31 slides
16
The Best in the Room — Superlatives for Executive HR
Naming the very best with superlative adjectives (short, long, irregular, and one of the) -- 31 slides
17
How Much, How Many — Quantifiers for HR Data
Talking about amounts with quantifiers (many/much, a few/a little, plenty of, not enough) -- 31 slides
18
Something Was Happening — Past Continuous for Storytelling
Telling HR stories with the past continuous (was/were + verb-ing), when/while, and interruptions -- 31 slides
19
You Should, You Could, You Might — Modal Verbs for HR Advice
Giving advice and weighing options with should, could, and might -- 31 slides
20
Block 2 Milestone — Fluency Review: From Data to Decisions
A milestone review of all Block 2 grammar in a data-to-decisions HR presentation -- 31 slides
08
How Much, How Many — Talking About Quantities in HR Reports and Workforce Data
Talking about quantities with quantifiers and countable/uncountable nouns -- 31 slides

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 1 — Who Is Murielle? — Your Professional Self-Introduction

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

Lawyers and executives in a top firm introduce themselves, state their roles, and meet new partners and clients. Observe how they say what they do and where they work. Direct connection to Lesson 1: professional self-introductions in a fast corporate world, close to your meetings with international executives.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Note 3 expressions the characters use to introduce themselves and their roles.

View on IMDb ↗
Podcast
The English We Speak (BBC) — The name of the game

A short (~2 min) BBC episode teaching the expression 'the name of the game' — the most important aspect of a situation — through a quick, natural professional exchange.

After listening, build one sentence of your own with 'the name of the game' about your job.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
All Ears English — How to Introduce Yourself Professionally in English

A practical lesson on professional self-introductions: name, role, company, and team, plus the questions you ask back. Connection to Lesson 1: ready-to-use sentence patterns for meetings, exactly the context you face with your Australian colleagues.

Tip: pause and repeat each introduction aloud. Practice your own version 3 times, and try one with an Australian-accent video to train your ear.

Watch on All Ears English ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 2 — This Is What I Do — Describing Your HR Role

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — How to design teams that don't suck

Organizational psychologist Adam Grant unpacks what makes teams work, using the 1980 'Miracle on Ice' hockey team to show how shared purpose beats raw individual talent.

Notice how guests describe what teams do and how they operate day to day.

Open episode ↗
Series
The Office (US) -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

A workplace comedy where every character explains their role and daily routine to the camera. Listen for how they describe what they do, who they report to, and what their department handles, all in the present simple. Direct connection to Lesson 2: describing roles, teams, and routines at work.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Note 3 sentences where a character describes their job with the present simple.
