View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Talking About Your Job in English" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on describing your job, your responsibilities, and your daily routine, with the present simple and frequency adverbs. Connection to Lesson 2: the exact structures you use to explain your HR role to an international colleague.

Tip: pause and build your own sentences with oversee, liaise with, handle, and report to. Try one with an Australian-accent clip to train your ear.

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 3 — Looking Back — Past HR Experiences & Career Milestones

Podcast
How I Built This with Guy Raz — Instagram: Kevin Systrom & Mike Krieger

Kevin Systrom and Mike Krieger tell the story of launching Instagram with a server that crashed every other hour, and how their photo-sharing app grew into one of the most-used platforms in the world.

Listen for how each founder describes the milestones of the company in sequence — great for career-story vocabulary.

Listen on Spotify ↗
Series
The Founder (2016 film) -- the story of how McDonald's grew

A business drama that follows a real career story full of milestones, a takeover, and big decisions. Listen for how the characters describe what happened, what was happening at the same time, and what they have achieved. Connection to Lesson 3: career milestones and telling a story across past tenses.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Note 3 sentences that describe a past milestone or a big change.

View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Past Simple vs Past Continuous & Present Perfect" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on how the three tenses work together: the past continuous for a longer background action, the past simple for a finished moment, and the present perfect for experience without a specific time. Connection to Lesson 3: the exact grammar you use to tell your career story.

Tip: pause and build your own sentences, like "While I was leading the merger, I spearheaded the downsizing" and "I have led three restructurings."

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 4 — What Is Happening Right Now — Describing Live Situations in Meetings

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — We All Hate Meetings, Here's How to Make Them Work

Kayak cofounder Paul English explains how the best companies run meetings that create clarity and better decisions. Leaders describe what teams are doing now, what is on track, and what is behind schedule — natural present continuous in real context.

Listen for present-continuous updates ('we're working on…', 'it's running behind').

Open episode ↗
Series
The Office (US) -- any meeting scene in the conference room

Watch a meeting unfold live: people are presenting, taking notes, reviewing ideas, and interrupting each other. Notice how characters narrate what is happening right now. Connection to Lesson 4: live action in meetings and the present continuous.

Tip: pause a meeting scene and describe it out loud: "Michael is talking, Jim is smiling, Pam is taking notes."

View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Present Continuous Tense -- am, is, are + verb-ing" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on the present continuous: how to form affirmatives, negatives, and questions for actions happening now, and how it is different from the present simple. Connection to Lesson 4: the exact grammar you use to describe a live situation.

Tip: pause and build your own present-continuous sentences with present, review, update, hire, and work.

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 5 — Every Day vs. Right Now — Mastering the Contrast That Confuses Everyone

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — The Science of Recharging on Weekends and Vacations

Adam Grant looks at the daily and weekly habits that actually help people recharge, and the routines high performers change to protect their energy.

Listen for present simple (routines) versus present continuous (what's changing right now).

Open episode ↗
Series
Ted Lasso -- a scene where a character describes the team's routine

Watch how characters talk about what they always do (training routine, meetings) versus what is happening right now during a specific week. Notice the switch between present simple and present continuous. Connection to Lesson 5: routine vs live action in a professional setting.

Tip: pause and describe one routine ("They usually train in the morning") and one live action ("Right now, they are warming up").

View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Present Simple vs Present Continuous" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on when to use the present simple (routines, facts, stative verbs) and when to use the present continuous (actions happening now), with the classic contrast and common mistakes. Connection to Lesson 5: the exact grammar you use to separate every day from right now.

Tip: pause and test the stative verbs -- try "I am knowing" and "I am preferring" out loud, then fix them to the present simple.

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 6 — Planning Ahead — Talking About Future HR Projects and Strategic Decisions

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — To Build Strategy, Start with the Future

Innosight's Mark Johnson explains "future-back" strategy: picturing where the company will be in 5-10 years and working backward to set today's goals, budgets and plans. Full of leaders describing what they are going to do next.

Every time a guest says "we're going to" or "we will," note the plan it describes.

Open episode ↗
Series
The Playbook (Netflix) -- any episode where a coach explains the plan

Coaches lay out their strategy and predict outcomes: what they are going to do and why they think it is going to work. Notice how "going to" signals decisions and predictions. Connection to Lesson 6: presenting goals, strategy, and forecasts.

Tip: pause after a plan and predict the result out loud: "This is going to work because..."

View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Going to for Future Plans and Predictions" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on "be going to": how to talk about plans already decided and predictions based on evidence, with negatives, questions, and common mistakes. Connection to Lesson 6: the exact grammar you use to plan ahead.

Tip: pause and build your own sentences with expand, deliver, set up, and launch.

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 7 — More, Less, the Best — Comparing HR Data and Business Performance

Podcast
Freakonomics Radio — Why Does the Richest Country in the World Have So Many Poor Kids?

The episode compares the U.S. with other wealthy nations and shows it has one of the highest child-poverty rates in the developed world, using rates, numbers and rankings to make sense of the gap.

Listen for comparatives and superlatives ("one of the highest," "more than," "the richest") used to interpret the data.

Open episode ↗
Series
Shark Tank -- any pitch where investors compare the numbers

Investors constantly compare revenue, margins, and growth: whose deal is better, which company is the most profitable. Notice the comparatives and superlatives in real business talk. Connection to Lesson 7: comparing performance data.

Tip: pause after a number and say a comparison out loud: "This margin is higher than the other company's."

View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Comparatives and Superlatives" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on comparatives and superlatives: short adjectives with -er/-est, long adjectives with more/the most, irregular forms, and common mistakes. Connection to Lesson 7: the exact grammar you use to compare data.

Tip: pause and build your own comparisons with efficient, cost-effective, and good.

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 8 — How Much, How Many — Talking About Quantities in HR Reports and Workforce Data

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — How to Get Forecasting Right

Forecaster Paul Saffo lays out six rules for planning around an uncertain future, using ranges of data and probabilities instead of a single guess. Lots of quantity and data language for planning conversations.

List every quantifier you hear (too many, a few, most, enough) and the noun it modifies.

Open episode ↗
Series
The Office (US) -- any episode with a staffing or budget discussion

The branch is always talking about how many people they need, how much work there is, and how little budget is left. Listen for quantifiers in real workplace talk: too many meetings, not enough time, a few clients. Connection to Lesson 8: quantities at work.

Tip: pause after a sentence and rebuild it: "There are too many..." / "There is not enough..." out loud.

View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Much, Many, A lot of, Few, Little" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on quantifiers and countable/uncountable nouns: much vs many, a few vs a little, too much vs enough, and the most common mistakes. Connection to Lesson 8: the exact grammar you use to describe workforce data.

Tip: pause and build your own sentences with applicants (countable) and feedback (uncountable).

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 8 — How Much, How Many — Talking About Quantities in HR Reports and Workforce Data

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — How to Get Forecasting Right

Forecaster Paul Saffo lays out six rules for planning around an uncertain future, using ranges of data and probabilities instead of a single guess. Lots of quantity and data language for planning conversations.

List every quantifier you hear (too many, a few, most, enough) and the noun it modifies.

Open episode ↗
Series
The Office (US) -- any episode with a staffing or budget discussion

The branch is always talking about how many people they need, how much work there is, and how little budget is left. Listen for quantifiers in real workplace talk: too many meetings, not enough time, a few clients. Connection to Lesson 8: quantities at work.

Tip: pause after a sentence and rebuild it: "There are too many..." / "There is not enough..." out loud.

View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Much, Many, A lot of, Few, Little" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on quantifiers and countable/uncountable nouns: much vs many, a few vs a little, too much vs enough, and the most common mistakes. Connection to Lesson 8: the exact grammar you use to describe workforce data.

Tip: pause and build your own sentences with applicants (countable) and feedback (uncountable).

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 9 — When It Was Happening — Describing Unfolding Situations in Meetings and Crisis Moments

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — Become friends with your rivals

Adam Grant tells stories of bitter workplace rivalries and how people turned tension and conflict into cooperation, describing what was happening when each conflict began.

Listen for the past continuous used to set the scene of each conflict.

Open episode ↗
Series
The Newsroom -- any scene during a breaking-news crisis in the control room

Watch a scene where a crisis unfolds and people describe what was happening: who was doing what when the incident started. Notice the past continuous for background actions. Connection to Lesson 9: describing an unfolding situation.

Tip: pause and narrate the scene: "While the team was working, the story broke."

View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Past Continuous vs Past Simple" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on the past continuous (was/were + verb-ing), the difference from the past simple, and how to use while and when for interrupted actions. Connection to Lesson 9: the exact grammar you use to describe an incident.

Tip: pause and build your own sentences with breakdown, dispute, and investigation.

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 10 — Advice, Possibility, and Professional Judgment — Using Modal Verbs in HR Conversations

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — How to love criticism

Adam Grant explores how leaders give and receive tough feedback and make hard people-decisions, from Bridgewater's radical candor to Pixar's feedback culture.

Listen for modals (should, could, must) used to advise on difficult decisions.

Open episode ↗
Series
The Office (US) -- any HR scene with Toby or Holly giving policy advice

Watch a scene where the HR representative advises Michael on what he should, could, or must do about an employee issue. Notice how modal verbs signal advice versus obligation. Connection to Lesson 10: professional judgment in HR conversations.

Tip: pause and rephrase the advice: "He should apologize. He must follow the policy."

View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Modal Verbs: should, could, must, might" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on modal verbs for advice, possibility, and obligation, and why the verb after a modal stays in the base form. Connection to Lesson 10: the exact grammar you use to give professional advice.

Tip: pause and build your own advice with policy, compliance, and appraisal.

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 11 — What's Happening Right Now — Present Continuous in Live Meeting Contexts

Podcast
The Modern Manager — 218: Facilitate Effective and Inclusive Meetings with Rae Ringel

Mamie Kanfer Stewart and facilitation expert Rae Ringel describe what a great host actually does during a live meeting—designing the space, alternating active and passive moments, and keeping every attendee engaged and heard.

Listen for the facilitation verbs ('facilitate', 'engage', 'pivot', 'draw people in') and try using one in your next meeting.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Series
Industry (HBO) -- any live trading-floor or meeting scene

Watch a fast, live scene where people describe what is happening around them in real time. Notice the present continuous for actions in progress: who is presenting, who is chiming in. Connection to Lesson 11: narrating a live meeting.

Tip: pause and narrate the scene: "She is presenting. They are chiming in."

View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Present Continuous Tense" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on the present continuous (am/is/are + verb-ing) for actions happening now, and why stative verbs like know and want stay in the present simple. Connection to Lesson 11: the exact grammar you use to narrate a live meeting.

Tip: pause and build your own sentences with facilitate, presenter, and breakout.

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 12 — Now vs. Always — Present Simple vs. Present Continuous in HR Conversations

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — Rethinking Flexibility at Work

Adam Grant examines what people usually do at work and what teams are changing right now about where, when and how they work.

Listen for present simple for stable routines and present continuous for ongoing change.

Open episode ↗
Series
The Office (US) -- an episode where someone covers a different role temporarily

Watch a scene where a character explains what they normally do and what they are doing this week while covering for someone. Notice the contrast between present simple (routine) and present continuous (temporary). Connection to Lesson 12: now versus always.

Tip: pause and say: "He usually works in sales, but this week he is covering reception."

View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Present Simple vs Present Continuous" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on when to use the present simple (routines and facts) and the present continuous (now and temporary), plus the frequency adverbs and stative verbs. Connection to Lesson 12: the exact contrast you use to describe your work.

Tip: pause and build sentences with delegate, cover, and reassign.

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 13 — Plans and Intentions — 'Going to' for Future Projects in HR

Podcast
HBR On Leadership — Getting Buy-In for Your Next Big Idea

Professor Sue Ashford and Ellen Bailey explain how to plan and pitch a new company initiative: framing the problem, mapping stakeholders, and building support so the idea actually gets launched.

Notice the future-plan phrases used to pitch what the company is going to change or allocate.

Open episode ↗
Series
The Office (US) -- a scene where a manager presents next year's plan

Watch a scene where a leader announces upcoming plans and intentions for the branch. Notice "going to" for decided plans and predictions. Connection to Lesson 13: presenting a project timeline and objective.

Tip: pause and say: "They are going to allocate more budget to training."

View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Going to for Future Plans and Predictions" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on "going to" for decided plans, intentions, and predictions based on evidence, plus the negative and question forms. Connection to Lesson 13: the exact grammar you use to present an HR project.

Tip: pause and build sentences with initiative, allocate, and rollout.

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 14 — Decisions and Offers — 'Will' for Spontaneous Choices in HR

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — Burnout is everyone's problem

Adam Grant shows why burnout is a shared responsibility and how colleagues can step in and support each other under pressure rather than leaving people to cope alone.

Listen for offers and reassurances with "will" ("I'll help you with that").

Open episode ↗
Series
Suits -- a scene where someone steps in to solve a sudden problem

Watch a scene where a character makes a quick decision to help and gives a promise. Notice "will" for offers and promises made in the moment. Connection to Lesson 14: reassuring and committing on the spot.

Tip: pause and say: "Do not worry, I will handle it."

View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Will vs Going To" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on "will" for spontaneous decisions, offers, and promises, and how it differs from "going to" for plans decided before. Connection to Lesson 14: choosing the right future form at work.

Tip: pause and build offers with volunteer, arrange, and confirm.

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 15 — Bigger, Better, Different — Comparatives in Global HR

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — The problem with all-stars

Adam Grant compares star-packed teams with balanced ones, using the Butler Bulldogs to show why more talent doesn't always mean better results.

Listen for comparatives (better, stronger, more talented) used to weigh teams.

Open episode ↗
Series
Suits -- a scene where two cases or offers are compared

Watch a scene where characters weigh two options and argue which is better. Notice comparatives in fast professional speech. Connection to Lesson 15: comparing and recommending.

Tip: pause and say: This option is better than the other.

View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Comparative Adjectives" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on short, long, and irregular comparatives, and how to use as ... as for equality. Connection to Lesson 15: comparing at work.

Tip: pause and build comparisons with bigger, more efficient, and better.

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 16 — The Best in the Room — Superlatives for Executive HR

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — Unlocking Hidden Potential with Malcolm Gladwell

Adam Grant and Malcolm Gladwell discuss what makes the best performers and highest-potential people stand out, and how the greatest talent is often hidden.

Listen for superlatives (the best, the most, the greatest) describing top performers.

Open episode ↗
Series
Suits -- a scene where the best lawyer or the top firm is named

Watch a scene where a character is called the best in the business. Notice superlatives in confident professional speech. Connection to Lesson 16: naming the standout performer.

Tip: pause and say: She is the most outstanding leader here.

View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Superlative Adjectives" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on short, long, and irregular superlatives, and how to use one of the + superlative. Connection to Lesson 16: describing the very best at work.

Tip: pause and build superlatives with the highest, the most efficient, and the best.

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 17 — How Much, How Many — Quantifiers for HR Data

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — When work takes over your life

Adam Grant looks at what happens when there is too much work and too little time, and how people who love their jobs can end up doing far too much.

Listen for quantifiers (too much, too many, not enough) used to talk about workload.

Open episode ↗
Series
Suits -- a scene about too many cases and not enough time

Watch a scene where the team has too much work and too few people. Notice quantifiers in fast professional speech. Connection to Lesson 17: talking about workload and resources.

Tip: pause and say: We have too many cases and not enough time.

View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Much, Many, A Lot Of" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on countable and uncountable nouns and how to use much, many, a little, and a few. Connection to Lesson 17: talking about data and quantities at work.

Tip: pause and build sentences with many roles and much budget.

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 18 — Something Was Happening — Past Continuous for Storytelling

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — Fixing the Trust Crisis with Rachel Botsman

Adam Grant sits down with trust expert Rachel Botsman to unpack why trust breaks down at work and in society, and how leaders rebuild it when a crisis hits. They walk through real situations where trust was being tested and how people responded in the moment.

Note the past-tense narration as guests describe what was happening during each breakdown of trust.

Open episode ↗
Series
Suits -- a scene describing what was happening during a chaotic day

Watch a scene where a character tells what everyone was doing when a problem hit. Notice the past continuous in fast professional speech. Connection to Lesson 18: narrating an incident.

Tip: pause and say: I was presenting when the system crashed.

View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Past Continuous" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on was/were + verb-ing and how to combine it with the past simple for interruptions. Connection to Lesson 18: telling stories about the past at work.

Tip: pause and build sentences with while and when.

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 19 — You Should, You Could, You Might — Modal Verbs for HR Advice

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — How to Rethink a Bad Decision

Adam Grant explores why we struggle to change course after a bad call and how to weigh options and give better advice on hard choices. He shares strategies for knowing when to stick with a decision and when to walk away.

Listen for modal verbs like should, could, and might as guests recommend and suggest courses of action.

Open episode ↗
Series
Suits -- a scene where someone gives advice on a difficult choice

Watch a scene where a character advises a colleague on a hard decision. Notice should, could, and might in fast professional speech. Connection to Lesson 19: advising on a dilemma.

Tip: pause and say: You should talk to them first.

View on IMDb ↗
YouTube
"Should, Could, Might" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on modal verbs for advice and possibility, and how to use them with the base verb. Connection to Lesson 19: recommending HR actions.

Tip: pause and build advice with should, could, and might.

Watch on BBC ↗

Complementary Activities

Extra materials to reinforce your learning outside class. Mark as done after watching or listening.

Lesson 20 — Block 2 Milestone — Fluency Review: From Data to Decisions

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — The Science of the Deal

Adam Grant breaks down evidence-based negotiation: how great negotiators make their case, compare offers and quantities, and reach agreements without sacrificing the relationship. Real examples show how leaders turn numbers into persuasive proposals.

Track the comparisons and numbers used to justify each offer and recommendation.

Open episode ↗
Series
Suits -- a scene where a lawyer presents a persuasive summary to win

Watch a scene where a character delivers a clear, persuasive summary under pressure. Notice how comparisons, superlatives, and recommendations combine. Connection to Lesson 20: a persuasive data-to-decisions pitch.

Tip: pause and say: Overall, this is our best option, and we should act now.

YouTube
"How to Give a Clear Presentation" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on structuring a short, persuasive presentation, from summary to conclusion. Connection to Lesson 20: presenting HR data with clarity.

Tip: pause and build a summary with overall, the best, and we should.

Watch on BBC ↗
